loop from Chapter 0 — every important journey is a standing A/B test with a measured
result, not an act of faith.

growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.title: Which journeys first, for your kind of startup
growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.text:
The order in §5.3 is the default; here's how it shifts by type:

* **Self-serve SaaS** — welcome/onboarding → dunning → win-back, exactly as written.
* **E-commerce** — abandoned-cart and browse-abandonment first (highest-ROI flows in
  commerce), then post-purchase and win-back. Klaviyo territory.
* **Consumer app** — habit/streak and resurrection dominate; onboarding is about
  forming the habit, not "setup."
* **Marketplace** — onboarding has *two* versions (supply and demand), and the key
  retention message is "your side of the market is active" (a new listing, a new buyer).
* **Community / web3** — the link-and-verify and gamification journeys (Chapter 8) *are*
  the lifecycle program; email supports them.
* **B2B with sales** — onboarding + expansion/upsell matter most; dunning if self-serve
  billing exists; lifecycle messaging assists the human, it doesn't replace them.

growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.text:
A few things worth settling while this chapter is fresh — each one makes the build in
Chapter 6 noticeably smoother:

1. **Pick the first journey** — welcome + onboarding, for the §5.3 reasons (with
   dunning next if you charge; it's the fastest revenue win in the list).
2. **Map each journey to its events** — the one that triggers it and the one that
   exits it. If the exit event is hard to name, that's usually a sign the journey
   doesn't have a clear goal yet.
3. **Sketch your cadence** per stage and a global frequency cap, so the pile-up
   failure from §5.5 can't happen by accident.
4. **Check the deliverability basics** — SPF/DKIM/DMARC on a sending subdomain and
   one-click unsubscribe (§5.6). Cheap to set up now; expensive to retrofit once a
   domain's reputation is damaged.
5. **Choose your tool deliberately** (§5.9) — and if it's code-first, the next
   chapter is the build.
6. **Plan a holdout** for that first journey, so its lift is measured from day one
   rather than argued about later.

You now know *what* to build and *why*. Next, you build it — in code.

***

***

## Go deeper

* [Customer.io — lifecycle automation & personalization guide](https://customer.io/learn/lifecycle-marketing/automation-and-personalization-guide) — the whole program as a five-step arc, from a tool built for exactly this.
* [Reforge — build your lifecycle marketing strategy](https://www.reforge.com/guides/build-your-lifecycle-marketing-strategy) — the retention-as-output framing §5.2 leans on.
* [Lenny Rachitsky — what is a good activation rate](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/what-is-a-good-activation-rate) — the activation benchmarks behind the "build onboarding first" argument.
* [Stripe — Smart Retries and revenue recovery](https://docs.stripe.com/billing/revenue-recovery/smart-retries) — the primary source on dunning mechanics.
* [Klaviyo — win-back email examples](https://www.klaviyo.com/blog/winback-email-campaign-examples) — flow structure and timing for resurrection, transferable beyond e-commerce.
* [Google — email sender guidelines](https://support.google.com/mail/answer/81126) — the actual inbox rules; everything else is commentary.
* [Resend — Gmail & Yahoo bulk sending requirements](https://resend.com/blog/gmail-and-yahoo-bulk-sending-requirements-for-2024) — the readable version of those rules.
* [Litmus — State of Email reports](https://www.litmus.com/state-of-email-reports) — benchmark data for calibrating open/click expectations without vendor spin.
* [Eppo — marketing incrementality 101](https://www.geteppo.com/blog/marketing-incrementality-101) — holdout test designs, one level deeper than §5.10.
* [Reforge — run a lock-down incrementality test](https://www.reforge.com/guides/method-run-a-lock-down-incrementality-test-on-meta-ads) — the same holdout logic applied to paid channels, previewing Chapter 7.
* [Userlist — behavioural email triggers](https://userlist.com/blog/behavior-email-campaign-triggers/) — an idea-bank of trigger → message pairs.

growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.title: 5 — Lifecycle messaging: which emails, in what order
growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.text:
> **In this chapter:** the lifecycle stages mapped to AARRR, the order to build them
> in (and why), why triggered messages beat batch-and-blast, how many touch-points is
> right, how to stay out of the spam folder, the landscape of tools (and when no-code is
> the right call), and how to measure impact honestly with holdouts. This is the
> strategy; Chapter 6 is the code.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into twelve short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. Four of them carry a short video, with a transcript and a distilled
digest underneath so the point sticks even if you don't watch. Work through them in
order, or jump to what you need:

1. [Lifecycle messaging plugs the bucket](./01-plugs-the-bucket.mdx) — the right message, triggered by the right behaviour, at the right time.
2. [The stages, mapped to AARRR](./02-stages-aarrr.mdx) — the eight programs and which stage each one serves.
3. [The order to build them — and why](./03-build-order.mdx) — welcome first, dunning early if you charge, then win-back.
4. [Triggered beats batch-and-blast](./04-triggered-beats-batch.mdx) — how a product event becomes a message, and why the gap is huge.
5. [Why touch-points matter](./05-touch-points.mdx) — multi-touch beats single-touch, and the dial you can turn too far.
6. [Deliverability](./06-deliverability.mdx) — none of this works if you don't land in the inbox.
7. [Personalise on behaviour](./07-personalise-on-behaviour.mdx) — `Hi {{first_name}}` is not personalisation.
8. [Multi-channel](./08-multi-channel.mdx) — email is the workhorse, not the only horse.
9. [The tools you could use](./09-the-tools.mdx) — the honest map, and when no-code is the right call.
10. [Prove it works: holdouts](./10-holdouts.mdx) — opens and clicks don't tell you what a message *caused*.
11. [Which journeys first](./11-which-journeys-first.mdx) — how the default order shifts by your kind of startup.
12. [Before you move on](./12-before-you-move-on.mdx) — what to settle now, then the quiz.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: The loop: event in, journey runs, event out
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
Hogsend's whole model is one loop:

1. An event arrives — from your backend (`POST /v1/ingest`), from a webhook source, or
   from PostHog. The same server-side events you send to PostHog in Chapter 3 are the
   events you send here.
2. The engine stores it, then routes it to every journey **subscribed to that event**.
3. The journey runs — it can wait days, check history, branch, send email, message
   Discord/Telegram — and fires its own events back (`journey:completed`, `email.*`,
   etc.) so the loop is measurable in PostHog.

The key property: **a journey is durable TypeScript.** `await ctx.sleep(...)` is a
real two-day pause that survives deploys and restarts — not a cron job, not a flowchart
approximating control flow. You write normal code; the engine makes it durable.

### Why "durable" is the whole game

It's worth understanding what that buys you, because it's the reason a code-first journey
engine exists at all. A lifecycle journey is mostly *waiting* — send, wait two days,
check, send, wait five days, check. Naively, you might reach for `setTimeout` or a
background `sleep`. That works until your server restarts mid-wait (a deploy, a crash, an
OOM) — and then every in-flight wait is lost, and every user mid-journey is stranded. You
can't run a messaging program on a process that forgets what it was doing every time you
ship.

Durable execution solves this: the engine persists the journey's position, so a
two-day sleep genuinely survives a redeploy on day one and resumes on day two as if
nothing happened. That's what lets you write a multi-day program as a single, readable
function instead of a pile of cron jobs and database flags reconstructing the state by
hand. The alternatives (later in this chapter) all exist to approximate this; Hogsend
gives it to you as plain control flow.

There's a subtlety inside "durable" that the exactly-once piece returns to: durability is
achieved by being able to *replay* a journey from the top after a crash and fast-forward
through the parts that already ran. That replay model is why exactly-once sending needs
engine support rather than hope — hold the thought.

### Send the trigger events

From your backend, the same place you call `posthog.capture(...)`, also post to Hogsend:

```ts
await fetch(`${HOGSEND_URL}/v1/ingest`, {
  method: "POST",
  headers: { "content-type": "application/json", authorization: `Bearer ${KEY}` },
  body: JSON.stringify({
    event: "user.created",
    userId: "user_123",
    userEmail: "sam@example.com",
    properties: { plan: "free" },
  }),
});
```

That `user.created` event is what triggers the welcome journey below. One server-side
emission, two consumers: PostHog measures it, Hogsend acts on it. And because it's
server-side, it's ad-blocker-proof (Chapter 3) — your lifecycle program never depends
on a browser script that a third of your users block.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: Anatomy of a journey
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
Every journey is `defineJourney({ meta, run })`. The `meta` declares *when* it runs and
the guardrails; `run` is the actual control flow.

```ts
import { days, hours } from "@hogsend/core";
import { defineJourney, sendEmail } from "@hogsend/engine";
import { Events, Templates } from "./constants/index.js";

export const example = defineJourney({
  meta: {
    id: "example",
    name: "Example journey",
    enabled: true,
    trigger: { event: Events.USER_CREATED },     // the event that starts it
    entryLimit: "once",                            // once | once_per_period | unlimited
    suppress: hours(12),                           // ignore re-triggers within this window
    exitOn: [{ event: Events.USER_DELETED }],      // events that end the journey early
  },
  run: async (user, ctx) => {
    // user: { id, email, stateId, journeyName, properties }
    // ctx:  durable primitives — sleep, history, waitForEvent, trigger, guard
  },
});
```

The pieces that matter for lifecycle work:

* **`trigger.event`** — the event the journey subscribes to. Add **`where`** to filter
  on properties: `trigger: { event: Events.USER_CREATED, where: (b) => b.prop("plan").eq("free") }`.
* **`entryLimit`** — `"once"` (a user can only ever enter once), `"once_per_period"`
  (with `entryPeriod`), or `"unlimited"`. This is your re-enrollment policy and an
  important correctness control.
* **`exitOn`** — the events that stop the journey immediately. **This is how you
  implement suppression from Chapter 5:** exit the upgrade sequence the instant the
  user upgrades. An exit fires even mid-wait — a journey parked on a 5-day
  `waitForEvent` aborts cleanly the moment an exit event arrives, so no post-wait
  side effects run.
* **`suppress`** — a debounce so a burst of the same trigger doesn't start multiple
  runs.

Inside `run`, you use the context primitives:

* `ctx.sleep({ duration, label })` — durable pause.
* `ctx.sleepUntil(at, { label })` — durable pause until an absolute instant.
* `ctx.when` — a timezone-aware scheduler for computing that instant: e.g.
  `ctx.when.tomorrow().at("09:00")` resolves 9am *in the user's own timezone* and
  respects your configured send window, so "send the day-2 email at breakfast, not at
  3am" is one line instead of timezone arithmetic.
* `ctx.history.hasEvent({ userId, event, within? })` → `{ found, count }` — did this
  already happen?
* `ctx.history.events({ userId, event, within, limit })` — pull the matching events to
  inspect.
* `ctx.waitForEvent({ event, timeout, label? })` — pause until *this user* emits an
  event or the timeout elapses; returns `{ timedOut, properties? }` (the matched
  event's payload, so you can branch on the answer). An optional `lookback` first
  checks recent history, covering the gap where the event fired a moment *before* the
  wait was established.
* `ctx.trigger({ event, userId, properties? })` — emit an event back into the pipeline.
* `ctx.guard.isSubscribed()` — re-check subscription after a long sleep.
* `ctx.checkpoint(label)` — mark progress so operators can see where users sit.

And you send with `sendEmail({ to, userId, journeyStateId, template, subject, props? })`,
where `template` is a key from your `src/emails/` registry (the next piece).

The thing to notice across all of this: it's *just code*. The branching is `if`. The wait
is `await`. The history check is a function call. There's no DSL to learn and no canvas
to fight — which is exactly why a journey can express logic a no-code tool can't, and why
it's reviewable in a pull request like the rest of your product.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: Templates: email as code
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
The journeys decide *when* and *who*; templates decide *what lands in the inbox*. In a
Hogsend app, templates are **React components** — the
[React Email](https://react.email/docs/introduction) model, where an email is a
component tree (`<Container>`, `<Button>`, `<Text>`) that renders to the
table-based HTML that email clients actually tolerate. If you've ever hand-written
`<td>` soup for Outlook, this is the upgrade: components, props, type-checked data, and
your design system's tokens — compiled to HTML that works in clients stuck in 2009.

**What to take from it:**

* The Vercel × Resend template wires an isolated API key into your project *mid-deploy*,
  so a fresh Next.js app ships with email hooked up from the first commit — no separate
  setup step.
* The email itself is a React Email component with typed props and Tailwind; Zeno's whole
  point is that you write ordinary React and get the Outlook/Gmail cross-client
  compatibility handled for you, instead of hand-writing inline styles and table soup.
* React Email's local dev server previews every template in mobile and desktop, shows the
  HTML *and* plain-text versions, and lets you fire a real test send — the exact
  preview-before-you-send loop this piece recommends.

A template in your app is a component plus a registry entry:

```tsx
// src/emails/activation-welcome.tsx
export function ActivationWelcome({ firstName, ctaUrl }: Props) {
  return (
    <EmailLayout preview="Let's get you set up">
      <Text>Welcome{firstName ? `, ${firstName}` : ""} 👋</Text>
      <Text>One thing to do today: create your first project.</Text>
      <EmailButton href={ctaUrl}>Create a project</EmailButton>
    </EmailLayout>
  );
}
```

The engine owns the pipeline from there: when a journey calls `sendEmail({ template:
Templates.ACTIVATION_WELCOME, props })`, the engine renders the component to HTML,
checks the user's preferences/unsubscribe state, rewrites links for first-party click
tracking, records the send, and only then hands plain HTML to the delivery provider
(Resend by default — [their docs](https://resend.com/docs/introduction) cover the wire;
[Vercel's guide](https://vercel.com/kb/guide/sending-emails-from-an-application-on-vercel)
shows the same stack outside Hogsend). Two consequences worth noticing:

* **Templates version with your code.** The welcome email that shipped with v1.4 is in
  the v1.4 git history — reviewable, revertable, diffable
  ([Resend's templates post](https://resend.com/blog/introducing-templates) makes the
  same versioning argument even for their hosted flavour).
* **Content vs plumbing stays split.** Journeys never concatenate HTML; templates never
  decide who gets mail. The same separation your product keeps between logic and view.

**What to take from it:**

* The problem React Email exists to solve: email clients — old *and* new (Gmail, Yahoo,
  Outlook, Superhuman, Notion Mail) — still don't support SVG, `border-radius`, and most
  modern CSS, so you need a component layer that emits cross-client, table-based HTML.
* The workflow is three steps — *code* the template (unstyled components + Tailwind +
  typed props that fail the build when missing), *test* it (preview across clients, a
  linter that flags broken and 404 links), then *send* it (a `render` util turns the
  component into plain HTML any provider accepts).
* The existing open-source options were stale (one's last commit was seven years ago;
  another's docs were badly outdated), which is exactly why a fresh component-model tool
  was worth building — the "email as code" case this piece makes.

**Test emails like code, too.** A template is about to sit in front of thousands of
sends, so it's worth actually looking at it first:
React Email's dev server previews every template with sample props; send-to-yourself
against [test addresses](https://resend.com/docs/dashboard/emails/send-test-emails)
(or a capture sandbox — [Mailtrap's testing guide](https://mailtrap.io/blog/how-to-test-email/)
covers the methodology) before pointing at real users; and keep
[transactional-email conventions](https://postmarkapp.com/guides/transactional-email-best-practices)
in mind — a lifecycle email is closer to a receipt than a brochure: one job, one CTA,
plain and fast.

**What to take from it:**

* React Email 4 adds a spam checker (it runs SpamAssassin, the standard open-source tool)
  that scores your email the way inboxes will and flags the phrases dragging the score
  down — catch deliverability problems in preview, not after a send.
* The linter surfaces accessibility and performance issues — a missing `alt` tag, an
  oversized image that slows the client — and links each finding straight to the offending
  line.
* The compatibility checker (backed by "Can I email") flags CSS that breaks in specific
  clients — `object-fit`, or `height` vs `min-height` diverging between Outlook and Yahoo —
  so the "Outlook breaks this" bug is caught in the preview instead of a customer's inbox.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: Welcome + onboarding (Activation)
A behavioural nudge series (Activation)
Win-back (Retention)
Dunning (Revenue)
Referral (Referral)
Correctness: exactly-once and re-enrollment
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
The failure mode that kills trust in a lifecycle program isn't a missed email — it's a
**double send**. Here's why it's a real risk and how the engine closes it.

Remember the loop: durability works by *replaying* a crashed journey from the top and
fast-forwarding through completed steps. Now imagine a worker dies **after** the
provider accepted your welcome email but **before** the engine recorded that step as
complete. The replay re-runs the send line. Without engine support, that user gets two
welcomes — and at scale, every deploy is a small chance of embarrassing some slice of
your users. Three things worth internalising:

* **Replay safety is automatic.** The engine derives a deterministic idempotency key
  for every `sendEmail()` and `ctx.trigger()` from the run's identity plus the nearest
  authored wait label, and dedupes on it — so a replayed send collapses into the
  original instead of delivering twice. You write nothing extra.
* **The one authoring rule:** if you send the **same template twice in one journey**
  (like the dunning reminder), give each site a distinct `idempotencyLabel` so
  their keys can't collide. Forgetting isn't silent — the engine throws a loud
  key-collision error in dev, which is exactly when you want to find out.
* **Keep the run deterministic where it matters.** If a *non-deterministic* choice
  picks what to send — an LLM choosing a template, a random variant — wrap it in
  `ctx.once(label, compute)`: the engine records the first result and replays it
  verbatim, so a crash can't re-roll the dice into a different-but-duplicate message.
  Same logic for timestamps: prefer the event's own `occurredAt` (from
  `ctx.waitForEvent`'s returned properties) over `new Date()`.

And separately from replays, **`entryLimit` is your re-enrollment policy** — it governs
legitimate *new* runs, not crashed ones. `"once"` for a welcome (a user signs up once).
`"once_per_period"` for win-back and dunning (they can lapse again, but not be spammed).
`"unlimited"` only for counting journeys (e.g. the Discord gamification in Chapter 8,
where every reaction must re-count).

This is the kind of correctness you get *because* the journey is code in a system built
for it — exactly-once delivery across redeploys is brutally hard to get right by hand,
and it's the subtlest of the reasons a purpose-built engine beats the roll-your-own
options in the next piece.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: Other ways you could build this (and why durable execution)
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
A growth team should know what it's choosing instead of. The ways teams build lifecycle
automation, roughly worst-to-best for this job:

* **`setTimeout` / in-process timers.** The tempting first reach, and the one that
  hurts: every wait is lost on the next deploy or crash. Fine for a 30-second
  debounce, catastrophic for a 3-day sequence.
* **Cron + a state column.** A nightly job scans the users table and decides who's due
  for which email. It works, and plenty of companies run on it — but you end up
  hand-rebuilding state, idempotency, and branching that the engine gives you for free,
  and the logic smears across cron jobs and SQL until no one can read the program.
  The tell: six months in, nobody can answer "what messages can user X possibly
  receive this week?" without reading four jobs and a spreadsheet.
* **A job queue with delayed jobs (BullMQ, Sidekiq).** Better — delays survive restarts.
  But you're still assembling sequences, exit conditions, and exactly-once yourself, job
  by job. Each journey becomes a distributed state machine you maintain by hand.
* **Durable-execution platforms (Temporal, Inngest, DBOS, Restate).** The right
  *category* — they make multi-step, long-running, restart-proof workflows a first-class
  thing. Hogsend uses one (Hatchet) under the hood. If you're building generic
  workflows, these are excellent; for lifecycle *messaging* specifically you'd still
  build the email/preferences/tracking/identity layer on top, which is what Hogsend is.
* **No-code automation (Zapier, n8n, Make).** Great glue for "when X, do Y" one-shots;
  weak for stateful multi-day per-user journeys with branching. Useful at the edges.

The summary: lifecycle messaging is a *durable, stateful, per-user* problem, and the
tools that fit it are the ones that treat long waits and exactly-once delivery as solved.
Hogsend's bet is that giving you that as ordinary TypeScript — plus the email, identity,
and tracking layers already wired — beats both the roll-your-own stack and the no-code
canvas for a team that codes.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: Feeding your journeys and proving lift
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
Your journeys are only as good as the events feeding them. Three sources:

* **Your backend** — `POST /v1/ingest` for product events (signup, payment, milestone).
  This is the primary source and it's server-side, so ad-blocker-proof (Chapter 3).
* **Webhook sources** — `defineWebhookSource()` turns any external webhook (Stripe,
  a form, a CRM) into an event that can trigger a journey. Declare the auth header,
  optionally validate the payload, and write one `transform()` that maps the
  provider's shape onto your event names — from then on a Stripe invoice failure is
  just another event in the same pipeline.
* **PostHog** — for *batch* / analytics-defined audiences (e.g. "did signup but not
  activate in 7 days"), build a PostHog cohort + a workflow webhook that posts to
  `/v1/ingest`. PostHog decides *who* needs action; Hogsend decides *what* to do. This
  is the bridge that powers inactivity-based win-back without Hogsend polling anything.

That last one is the clean expression of "PostHog detects, Hogsend acts" (Chapter 0):
the thing that's hard to compute in real time (30-day dormancy across all users) is a
cohort PostHog already maintains; Hogsend just receives the verdict and runs the program.

### The holdout hook

Chapter 5 said: prove lift with a holdout. Because enrollment is just code, you can
implement a campaign holdout directly — deterministically bucket a small fraction of
users out of enrollment and compare their downstream events to the enrolled group:

```ts
run: async (user, ctx) => {
  // ~10% holdout, deterministic per user so it's stable across replays.
  if (hashToUnitInterval(user.id) < 0.1) {
    await ctx.trigger({ event: "journey.held_out", userId: user.id });
    return; // measured, not messaged
  }
  // ...normal journey...
}
```

Two details are load-bearing. The bucketing is a **hash of the user id**, not
`Math.random()` — deterministic, so a replay makes the same decision and the same user
is always in the same bucket across every journey version. And the held-out path still
**fires an event** — the control group must be *visible* in PostHog, or you'll have
nothing to compare against.

Then in PostHog, compare activation/retention/revenue for `journey.held_out` users vs
enrolled users. That difference is your real, causal lift — the growth-team experiment
loop (Chapter 0) running continuously, in production, on the journey you just shipped.
When Chapter 9's monthly review asks "what's the lifecycle program actually worth?",
this hook is where the answer comes from.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
The build order that tends to work, if you want a checklist for the week:

1. **Wire your backend** to `POST /v1/ingest` for `user.created`, `payment.failed`,
   and your milestone event — every journey below hangs off these three.
2. **Build `activation-welcome` first**; add its templates to `src/emails/` and
   preview them in the React Email dev server before they send (the templates piece) — a
   broken layout is much cheaper to catch in the preview than in a customer's inbox.
3. **Add dunning if you charge**, then **win-back** — the same order as Chapter 5,
   for the same reasons.
4. **Put a holdout on that first journey** and watch the lift in PostHog — so when
   someone asks whether the program works, there's a number instead of a feeling.

You've now plugged the bucket. The rest of the course opens the tap — and makes sure
every drop lands in an audience you own.

## Go deeper

* [React Email — documentation](https://react.email/docs/introduction) — the component model your `src/emails/` templates use.
* [Resend — documentation](https://resend.com/docs/introduction) — the delivery API underneath the default provider.
* [Vercel — sending emails from an application](https://vercel.com/kb/guide/sending-emails-from-an-application-on-vercel) — the same React Email + Resend stack assembled outside Hogsend, useful for seeing what the engine automates.
* [Resend — introducing Templates](https://resend.com/blog/introducing-templates) — the version-controlled-templates argument, from the provider's side.
* [Postmark — transactional email best practices](https://postmarkapp.com/guides/transactional-email-best-practices) — the one-job-one-CTA discipline lifecycle emails should copy.
* [Resend — send test emails](https://resend.com/docs/dashboard/emails/send-test-emails) — sandbox addresses for exercising flows safely.
* [Mailtrap — how to test email](https://mailtrap.io/blog/how-to-test-email/) — capture-sandbox methodology for staging environments.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: 6 — Building the lifecycle in Hogsend
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
> **In this chapter:** how an event becomes a running journey, why durable execution
> matters (and what breaks without it), the anatomy of `defineJourney`, email templates
> as code, working examples for each lifecycle stage, the other ways you could build
> this, and the correctness rules that stop you double-sending. Code here mirrors real
> production journeys.

If you'd like to follow along, the examples assume a Hogsend app —
`pnpm dlx create-hogsend@latest` scaffolds one in a minute.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into twelve short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. One piece (Templates) carries three short videos on building email as
React components: read the one-line reason it's worth your time, watch it (or read the
transcript underneath), then read the digest. Work through them in order, or jump to what
you need:

1. [The loop: event in, journey runs, event out](./01-the-loop.mdx) — how an event becomes a durable running journey, and why "durable" is the whole game.
2. [Anatomy of a journey](./02-anatomy-of-a-journey.mdx) — `defineJourney`, its meta guardrails, and the context primitives.
3. [Templates: email as code](./03-templates-email-as-code.mdx) — React components that render to inbox-proof HTML, versioned with your code.
4. [Welcome + onboarding](./04-welcome-onboarding.mdx) — the first journey: welcome now, branch on behaviour two days later.
5. [A behavioural nudge series](./05-behavioural-nudge-series.mdx) — sleep, check, send only if needed — the shape most onboarding takes.
6. [Win-back](./06-win-back.mdx) — `waitForEvent` gives a dormant user a window to return before the final nudge.
7. [Dunning](./07-dunning.mdx) — the highest-ROI journey: recover a failed charge, exit the moment payment clears.
8. [Referral](./08-referral.mdx) — ask at peak satisfaction, and only ask users who are still active.
9. [Correctness: exactly-once](./09-exactly-once.mdx) — how the engine stops a replay from double-sending, and the one rule you author.
10. [Other ways you could build this](./10-other-ways.mdx) — cron, job queues, durable-execution platforms, no-code — and why durable wins here.
11. [Feeding your journeys and proving lift](./11-triggers-and-holdout.mdx) — where the events come from, and the holdout that measures your impact.
12. [Before you move on](./12-before-you-move-on.mdx) — the build order for the week, then a quiz to check yourself.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: Where are you starting from?
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
A gentle reminder on order before anything else: this chapter is deliberately seventh.
If your retention curve (Chapter 4) still decays to zero, this chapter will serve you
much better after Chapters 5–6 have done their work — paid acquisition into a leaky
bucket is, as Chapter 2 put it, buying churn. Teams that prove the bucket holds first
get dramatically more out of every pound they spend here.

The rest of this chapter reads differently depending on where you're starting from, so
it's worth pausing on your current mix before the tactics. A team that's all-organic is
really asking whether paid is worth *starting*; a team that's mostly-paid tends to need
the measurement and owned-audience work first, because every tracking gap and every
rented audience is already costing money.

Two facts frame everything that follows:

* **Channels decay.** Andrew Chen's
  [law of shitty clickthroughs](https://andrewchen.com/the-law-of-shitty-clickthroughs/)
  is the empirical observation that every marketing channel's performance degrades as
  it saturates — banner ads went from 78% CTR in 1994 to ~0.05%, and every channel
  since has followed the same curve. Whatever works for you now will work less well
  per dollar next year. The counter isn't channel-hopping; it's capturing what a
  channel brings you into something you own (Chapter 8) while the channel still works.
* **Early traction rarely comes from paid at all.** Paul Graham's
  [Do Things that Don't Scale](https://paulgraham.com/ds.html) is the canonical
  argument: almost all startups get their first users manually — recruiting one at a
  time, launching in communities, doing unscalable favours — and the founders who wait
  for ads to do it stall. If you're pre-revenue, this chapter's job is mostly to stop
  you spending money you shouldn't spend yet.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: The channel menu — and how to choose
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
Meta is this chapter's worked example because it's the most common paid channel and its
tracking story teaches the privacy lessons every channel shares. But a growth team picks
the channel that fits its product, and for many readers of this course Meta is *not*
the first choice:

* **Google Ads (Search).** Intent-based — you reach people *already searching* for the
  solution. For products with clear search demand (someone Googling "invoice software"),
  this often converts far better than interrupting a feed. Has its own server-side
  conversion path (Enhanced Conversions, the Google Ads API).
* **TikTok / Reddit / X ads.** TikTok for consumer and younger demographics; Reddit for
  niche/technical communities (it can work surprisingly well for dev tools in the right
  subreddits); X for tech/founder audiences. Each has its own pixel + a server-side
  events API with the same dedup logic as §7.7.
* **LinkedIn Ads.** Expensive per click, but the only platform with reliable job-title/
  company targeting — the B2B choice when your buyer is defined by role.
* **The non-paid channels that often beat paid for this audience:**
  * **Content / SEO** — compounding, owned, and the canonical growth engine for dev
    tools and B2B (it's *why* Supabase, PostHog itself, and others grew). Slow to
    start, nearly free to sustain. Rob Walling's rule of thumb for bootstrapped SaaS
    is that content is a 6–12-month investment before it pays — budget the patience,
    not just the writing.
  * **Launches** — [Show HN](https://news.ycombinator.com/showhn.html) (the actual
    rules are worth reading first: no marketing-speak, the thing must be tryable),
    Product Hunt, and the
    subreddit/newsletter/Discord ecosystem of your niche. Launches are spikes, not
    engines — their real value is seeding the loop: every launch visitor you capture
    into an owned channel (Chapter 8) keeps paying after the spike dies.
  * **Community** — Discord/Reddit/Slack communities, the subject of Chapter 8. For
    community-led and web3 products this *is* the acquisition channel.
  * **Partnerships / integrations / marketplaces** — listing in the ecosystem your
    users already live in (a Shopify app, a Vercel integration, a Raycast extension).
    You inherit the platform's traffic and its trust.

**What to take from it:**

* Content marketing fails without a framework for *what to write first*: Walling's own
  early spend flopped because every piece was generic top-of-funnel ("email marketing
  myths") with no plan for sequencing.
* Map content to the five stages of customer awareness (unaware → problem-aware →
  solution-aware → product-aware → most-aware) and **start at the bottom** — versus
  pages, alternative-to pages, case studies, feature docs — because that content closes
  sales *and* works its way up the funnel.
* Match content to search intent: informational/commercial/transactional keywords line
  up with the stages, and tools like Semrush and Similarweb tag that intent so you can
  prioritise which terms to chase.

### A note for dev-tool, community, and web3 founders

For **developer tools and B2B, paid social is weak** — content, community, and product-
led growth usually out-perform it. Developers install ad blockers at the highest rate
of any audience, distrust interruption marketing, and discover tools through
[GitHub, Hacker News, docs, and each other](https://www.heavybit.com/library/article/developer-marketing-guide/)
rather than feeds. Heavybit's
[developer-first go-to-market material](https://www.heavybit.com/devguild/developer-first-go-to-market)
and First Round's
[Retool case study](https://review.firstround.com/retools-path-to-product-market-fit-lessons-for-getting-to-100-happy-customers-faster/)
both land on the same shape: bottom-up, user-to-user distribution, seeded by content
and community, with paid as (at most) an amplifier.

**What to take from it:**

* Developers want a free tier to try before they adopt — they're skeptics who go
  straight to the *docs*, not the marketing pages, and for a developer tool the product
  itself *is* the developer experience.
* Developer marketing is showing the art of the possible, then teaching them to build it
  themselves; devs are late-adopter skeptics who often need ~10 touches before they try
  a tool, so trust compounds over repeated exposure.
* The reliable loop is talk to developers → ask "five whys" → fix what frustrates them
  *fast* → and critically **tell them** you fixed it — the step most teams skip.

And **web3/crypto ads face policy restrictions** on Meta and Google. For both segments,
treat paid as *fuel for the loop*, not the loop itself — which makes the owned channels
in Chapter 8 disproportionately important, and means the accuracy work in the rest of
this chapter applies mostly to whatever *small* paid spend you do run. **If you're a
dev tool, the useful way to read this chapter isn't "now go buy Meta ads" — it's
"here's how paid works and how to measure it honestly when you do use it," with most
of your energy pointed at Chapter 8.**

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: The maths that decides: CAC, LTV, payback
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
Whatever the channel, the same three numbers decide whether it's working. The
definitions matter — a16z's
[16 Startup Metrics](https://a16z.com/16-startup-metrics/) exists because founders
routinely get them wrong in ways that flatter the business:

* **CAC (customer acquisition cost)** — *fully loaded* spend to acquire one paying
  customer: ad spend plus the content, tools, and people behind it, divided by
  customers acquired. The classic self-deception is reporting CAC on ad spend alone,
  or on signups instead of customers.
* **LTV (lifetime value)** — the **gross-margin** contribution of a customer over their
  lifetime, where lifetime comes from your churn rate (roughly, monthly margin ÷
  monthly churn). Computing LTV on revenue instead of margin is the second classic
  self-deception — a16z calls both out by name.
* **Payback period** — months of gross margin to recover CAC. For a cash-constrained
  startup this is often the number that matters most: an LTV:CAC of 5 is cold comfort
  if payback takes three years and the bank account empties in one.

The folk benchmark you'll hear is **LTV:CAC ≥ 3 and payback under ~12 months** for
SaaS — better treated as a sanity range than a law; what actually protects you is
knowing your numbers and their trend. Two habits follow from that:

* **CAC is most useful per channel, not blended.** A blended CAC averages your free
  organic users into the paid number, which can hide a channel that's quietly
  unprofitable. Blended CAC is a board metric; per-channel CAC is the operating one.
  (Chapter 9's monthly ritual reviews blended CAC precisely to see whether
  owned/referral growth is pulling it down over time.)
* **LTV is worth segmenting by cohort and source.** Users from different channels
  retain differently — paid-social users commonly churn faster than organic (they had
  less intent). If paid cohorts' LTV is half your organic LTV, your real paid LTV:CAC
  may be under water while the blended number looks fine.

**What to take from it:**

* LTV falls out of ARPA and churn: average revenue per account ÷ churn rate, then taken
  down to margin — a $100 ARPA at 10% monthly churn is a $1,000 lifetime value, which is
  the ceiling on what you can spend to acquire that customer.
* Track **gross** revenue retention (churn + contraction only), not just the flashier
  net revenue retention — net can exceed 100% on expansion and quietly hide the bad news
  gross retention would show you.
* MRR is not cash flow (a $1,200 annual deal is $100 of MRR), and segmenting churn and
  CAC by customer size or source tells you far more than the blended number.

Where do the numbers come from? The machine you already built: revenue events are
server-side in PostHog (Chapter 3), source/UTM landed as person properties (§7.8), so
"LTV by acquisition source" is a PostHog insight, not a spreadsheet safari.

Benchmarks are worth less than your own numbers, so put them in. Watch the payback
figure most when cash is tight — a healthy LTV:CAC with a payback longer than your
runway still sinks you.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: The shape of a Meta campaign
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
From here to §7.9, the paid-social worked example. Meta (Facebook/Instagram) ads have a
fixed three-tier structure, and it's worth getting comfortable with it first because
everything else hangs off it:

* **Campaign** — sets the **objective** and budget strategy. One objective per campaign.
* **Ad Set** — the levers: **audience, placements, the optimisation event, budget,
  schedule**.
* **Ad** — the creative the user sees (image/video, copy, CTA, destination).

The single most consequential choice is the **campaign objective**, because it tells
Meta's delivery algorithm *which kind of person to go find*. Meta consolidated its old
objectives into six (ODAX):

1. **Awareness** — reach/recall, top of funnel.
2. **Traffic** — cheap clicks to a page. *Not* optimised for conversions — a common
   beginner trap is to use Traffic and wonder why clicks don't convert. Traffic buys you
   visitors who clicked; Sales buys you visitors who *act*. They are different people.
3. **Engagement** — interactions, video views, messages.
4. **Leads** — form fills, newsletter signups.
5. **App Promotion** — installs and in-app events.
6. **Sales** — purchases / highest-intent conversions.

**The rule of thumb worth carrying:** optimise for the event closest to revenue that
you can feed Meta *enough of*. The algorithm needs roughly **50 conversions per ad set
per week** to exit its "learning phase" and deliver efficiently. If you can't generate
50 purchases a week yet, a higher-funnel event (lead or signup) as the optimisation
target — with the deeper conversion passed back server-side (§7.7) — keeps the
algorithm fed. The most common early pattern goes the other way: optimising for
`purchase` at five a week starves the algorithm of signal, and it never learns who to
find.

Two practical corollaries of the learning phase:

* **Edits reset learning.** Every significant change (budget, audience, creative) can
  send the ad set back to school. The pattern that hurts — check daily, worry, edit —
  keeps it permanently in learning and permanently expensive. Weekly changes, on the
  cadence from Chapter 9, give the system time to settle.
* **Fewer, bigger ad sets learn faster.** Five ad sets splitting 10 conversions a week
  will all starve; one ad set receiving all 50 can learn. Consolidation is the modern
  default.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: The three audiences
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
Every ad set targets one of three kinds of audience:

* **Broad / interest ("cold").** Demographics and interests, or — increasingly — fully
  broad "Advantage+" audiences where you let Meta's ML find people. The 2024–2026 trend
  is clear: **broad targeting + strong creative + a good conversion signal now beats
  hand-built interest stacks**, because the algorithm does the targeting. Interest
  targeting is more of a hint than a filter now — which shifts the work from audience-
  picking to *creative* and *signal quality* (§7.7).
* **Custom Audiences ("warm", first-party).** Built from *your* data: a customer-list
  upload, site/app visitors, or events sent via the Conversions API. These are the
  highest-signal inputs you can give Meta — and they only exist because you captured
  first-party identity, which is the whole thesis of Chapter 8.
* **Lookalike Audiences ("prospecting").** Meta finds new people resembling a **seed**
  custom audience. **Seed quality dominates the result** — your *best* customers
  (recent purchasers, high-LTV) make a far better seed than "everyone who ever signed
  up." A 1% lookalike is the tightest match; widening to 3–10% trades match for scale.

### The full funnel

A standard structure layers these:

* **Top (cold):** Awareness/Traffic/Video to broad audiences. Cheap short video both
  earns reach *and* manufactures the video-viewer/engager audiences you'll retarget.
* **Middle (warm):** retarget site visitors, video viewers, engagers with
  trust/education content.
* **Bottom (hot):** retarget pricing-page visitors, trial-starters, cart abandoners
  with a direct offer.

> A starting split you'll see quoted is ~50–60% cold / 20–30% warm / 15–20% hot
> *(directional heuristic, not gospel)*. The pattern that catches small founders is
> over-investing in retargeting too early — a tiny warm pool can't scale spend. The
> warm pool grows by capturing more first-party identity, which is Chapter 8.

And a word on the part this course can't do for you: **creative is now the targeting.**
With broad audiences, the ad itself selects the audience — a video about "deploy
previews for your Next.js app" finds developers because only developers watch it. The
practical implication: you'll likely need more creative variants than feels natural
(winners fatigue in weeks), and the first three seconds are where the qualification
happens.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: Your conversion tracking is lying to you
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
Here's the part that trips up everyone who just installs the Meta Pixel and trusts the
numbers.

The **Meta Pixel** is browser-side JavaScript. It fires from the user's browser and
depends on cookies and the request actually completing. A stack of things now breaks
that:

* **iOS App Tracking Transparency** — most iOS users decline cross-app tracking *(opt-in
  is commonly cited around 20–25%, varies a lot by source and region — directional)*.
* **Safari/Firefox/Brave** cap or expire the cookies the Pixel relies on.
* **Ad blockers** drop the Pixel outright for ~10–30% of users (the same blocking you
  met in Chapter 3).
* **Third-party cookie deprecation** — the date keeps slipping, but the direction is
  certain.

The result: the Pixel under-counts conversions, Meta's algorithm optimises on partial
data, and your reported ROAS is wrong. You can't fix bid strategy on numbers that are
quietly missing a third of reality. This is the same lesson as Chapter 3's ad-blocker
section, now with money attached: browser-only measurement under-counts, and the fix is
to move the important events server-side.

The knock-on effect is worse than bad reporting: **the algorithm optimises on what it
sees.** If the Pixel disproportionately loses conversions from privacy-conscious,
ad-blocking users (it does), Meta learns to find *fewer people like them* — a silent
bias in delivery, not just a counting error. Signal quality is targeting quality.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: The fix: Pixel and Conversions API
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
The **Conversions API (CAPI)** sends events **server-side** — from your server (or a
tool/CDP) directly to Meta's endpoint, skipping the browser entirely. Because it never
touches the user's browser, it survives ad blockers, cookie expiry, and failed Pixel
loads.

Meta's own guidance is not "CAPI instead of Pixel" — it's **both, deduplicated.** You
send the same event from the browser (Pixel) and the server (CAPI), and Meta merges
them. Redundancy plus better matching.

### What server-side **does** fix

* Recovers web conversions lost to blockers, ITP, and failed Pixel loads.
* **Higher match quality** — your server has the user's hashed email/phone that the
  browser often doesn't.
* Lets you send **back-office conversions the browser never sees**: trial→paid,
  qualified-lead, refunds, LTV, offline events. This is huge — these are usually your
  *most valuable* conversions, and the Pixel can't see any of them.

### What server-side does **not** fix (worth being precise — this is where a lot of advice overpromises)

* **It is not a consent loophole.** You still need a lawful basis to collect, hash, and
  share user data (§7.9). A SHA-256-hashed email is **pseudonymous, not anonymous** —
  same input, same hash, re-identifiable.
* **It does not restore iOS *app-install* attribution.** That's governed by Apple's
  SKAdNetwork plus Meta's Aggregated Event Measurement — aggregated, delayed, modelled,
  *not* user-level. Web CAPI doesn't bring back per-user iOS attribution.
* **It doesn't fix bad inputs.** CAPI with no email/phone/external\_id matches poorly and
  barely helps. Garbage in, garbage matched.
* **It doesn't dedupe by magic** — see below.

> ⚠️ **A dated claim you'll still run into: the AEM "8 prioritised events" limit.**
> Meta removed the 8-event cap and manual configuration around **June 2025** and now
> [auto-aggregates eligible events](https://www.conversios.io/blog/meta-aggregated-event-measurement/).
> Older blogs and courses still teach the cap — if a guide mentions it, that's a good
> sign the rest is stale too.

### Deduplication (the thing people get wrong)

When an event goes out from both Pixel and CAPI, both copies need the **same
`event_id`** (and matching `event_name`) — Meta dedupes on `event_id` and counts it
once. Without it, a hybrid setup **double-counts** conversions, corrupting the very
ROAS it was built to measure. This is the single most common CAPI mistake, and it's
silent until you notice the numbers look too good.

### Event Match Quality (EMQ)

Meta scores each event source 0–10 on how well its data matches real Meta users. Aim
for **6 or higher** (8+ is better). The score goes up with more and better identifiers,
roughly weighted:

* **Strongest:** email, the click ID (`fbc`, from the `fbclid` on the landing URL),
  Facebook Login ID.
* **Medium:** phone, the browser ID (`fbp`), and your stable **`external_id`** (your
  user/contact id).
* **Weaker:** name, city, IP, user agent.

All PII must be normalised (trim, lowercase) and **SHA-256 hashed** before sending. The
way to raise EMQ is to **feed CAPI from where you actually have identity** — your
backend/CRM, which holds the email and phone the browser never sees. Which is exactly
where PostHog and Hogsend come in.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: Feeding it from PostHog (and Hogsend)
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
You don't have to hand-build a CAPI integration. PostHog's **Data Pipelines** include a
**Meta Ads conversions destination**:

* You give it your **Pixel/Dataset ID and access token**. PostHog **SHA-256-hashes**
  the email/name, and sends `event_name`, a unique `event_id` (enabling Pixel dedup),
  `event_time`, plus `fbc`/`fbclid` and user agent.
* Use the **same Pixel ID** for the browser Pixel and the PostHog destination so
  deduplication works.
* Filter the destination to **conversion events only** — you don't want every pageview
  going to Meta.

PostHog also auto-captures `fbclid`/`gclid` and UTMs as person properties and stitches
anonymous → identified when the user gives an email, so a later server-side conversion
carries the identifiers Meta needs for a high EMQ.

**Custom audiences without CSVs:** build a PostHog **cohort** (e.g. "closed-won in the
last 180 days") and sync it as a Meta custom audience — for retargeting, or as a
lookalike seed. No manual list upload, and it stays fresh.

**Where Hogsend fits:** Hogsend's events are server-side and keyed to its own identity
graph (email ↔ Discord ↔ Telegram ↔ anonymous). That means Hogsend can fire **enriched,
deep-funnel conversions** — trial→paid, qualified-lead — **server-side, with strong
identity**, which is exactly the high-EMQ, browser-invisible conversion that makes CAPI
worth it. The honest caveat (same as Chapter 3): this makes your *conversions* accurate
and your *journey triggers* ad-blocker-proof; it does **not** fix the user's client-side
PostHog analytics loss, and it does not override consent or ATT.

### Other ways to send server-side conversions

PostHog's destination is the path this course recommends because you're already in
PostHog, but it's not the only one — the field is worth knowing:

* **Stape / server-side Google Tag Manager** — a popular dedicated server container that
  forwards to Meta, Google, TikTok, etc. More control, more setup.
* **Segment / RudderStack** — if you already run a CDP, it has Meta/Google destinations.
* **Direct CAPI integration** — call Meta's endpoint from your backend yourself. Most
  control, most maintenance; only worth it if the managed options can't express what you
  need.

The principle is the same regardless of tool: **send from where you have identity,
dedupe on `event_id`, send only consented conversion events.**

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: Consent — the part that isn't optional
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
Server-side does not mean consent-free, and the rules tightened recently. It's worth
knowing exactly where the lines sit:

* As of **January 2025**, Meta requires **explicit user consent** before you upload
  contact info for custom audiences or send PII via CAPI.
* When you upload a list, **you are the data controller** —
  [Meta's custom-audience terms](https://www.facebook.com/legal/terms/customaudience)
  say so explicitly — and need a lawful basis (usually consent). A German regulator has
  ruled list uploads without consent unlawful, and — again — **hashing does not make
  data anonymous** in law.
* Meta's consent signals / data-processing options exist for exactly this — and in
  consent regimes, events for non-consenting users are best not sent at all.
* **Less is safer:** sending only what you need (hashed email/phone/external\_id) keeps
  you out of trouble. As of **September 2025**, Meta blocks custom conversions
  containing sensitive (health/financial) data.

The strategic point this sets up: an **owned, consented identity graph** — emails
captured *with opt-in* at signup and verification — is what makes the whole custom-
audience + CAPI + lookalike machine both *effective* and *compliant*. You can only feed
Meta good, legal data if you collected it well in the first place. That's Chapter 8.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/1.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/1.text:
A few things worth doing while this chapter is fresh:

1. **Settle whether paid social is even your channel** (§7.2). For a dev tool or
   community product, the honest answer is usually "mostly no" — in which case Chapter
   8 is where your energy goes, with paid kept to small tests.
2. **Write down your per-channel CAC and payback** for whatever you're doing today
   (§7.3), even roughly. A new channel can only be judged against a baseline, and most
   teams discover they don't have one.
3. If Meta is your channel: the **Meta Pixel** on your site (browser side) is step one…
4. …and the **PostHog → Meta Ads** destination with the **same Pixel ID**, filtered to
   conversion events, is step two — that's the dedup working for you.
5. It's worth checking your conversions carry **email + `fbc` + `external_id`** — that's
   what gets EMQ to 6 or higher, and EMQ is targeting quality.
6. **One campaign** is the right amount to start with: a Sales/Leads objective, broad
   audience, strong creative, optimising on the deepest event you can feed 50/week.
7. And wherever you collect email, **opt-in consent** at that moment is what makes the
   whole audience machine in §7.8 legal to run.

With traffic flowing and honestly measured, the last piece is the one that compounds:
turning that traffic into an audience you never have to pay to reach again.

***

***

## Go deeper

* [a16z — 16 Startup Metrics](https://a16z.com/16-startup-metrics/) — the canonical CAC/LTV definitions, including the exact ways founders fool themselves.
* [Andrew Chen — The Law of Shitty Clickthroughs](https://andrewchen.com/the-law-of-shitty-clickthroughs/) — why every channel decays, and what that implies for owning your audience.
* [Paul Graham — Do Things that Don't Scale](https://paulgraham.com/ds.html) — the case that early traction is manual, not paid.
* [Heavybit — Developer Marketing Guide](https://www.heavybit.com/library/article/developer-marketing-guide/) — where developers actually discover tools, from the fund that specialises in them.
* [Heavybit — Developer-First Go-to-Market](https://www.heavybit.com/devguild/developer-first-go-to-market) — bottom-up distribution mechanics for dev tools.
* [First Round — Retool's Path to Product-Market Fit](https://review.firstround.com/retools-path-to-product-market-fit-lessons-for-getting-to-100-happy-customers-faster/) — a real dev-tool's first-100-customers playbook, no paid ads in sight.
* [First Round Review — Go-to-Market hub](https://review.firstround.com/articles/go-to-market/) — a library of channel and positioning essays worth raiding.
* [Show HN — the official rules](https://news.ycombinator.com/showhn.html) — read before launching; the norms are the strategy.
* [Meta — Conversions API docs](https://developers.facebook.com/docs/marketing-api/conversions-api/) — the primary source for §7.7.
* [PostHog — Meta Ads destination](https://posthog.com/docs/cdp/destinations/meta-ads) — the managed CAPI path this course recommends.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic.title: 7 — Driving traffic
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic.text:
> **In this chapter:** how to choose an acquisition channel deliberately (and why paid
> social is the wrong first channel for many readers of this course), the CAC/LTV maths
> that decides whether a channel is working, the structure of a Meta campaign, the three
> audience types, why Pixel-and-Conversions-API (not Pixel alone), what server-side
> tracking does and doesn't fix, and how PostHog/Hogsend feed conversions and audiences
> back to the ad platform. Accuracy matters here — the privacy landscape is full of
> half-truths.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into ten short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. Where a piece has a video, the idea is the same each time: read the
one-line reason it's worth your time, watch it (or read the transcript underneath), then
read the short digest so the point sticks. Work through them in order, or jump to what you
need:

1. [Where are you starting from?](./01-where-you-start.mdx) — your acquisition mix changes how the rest reads; and channels decay.
2. [The channel menu](./02-channel-menu.mdx) — pick the channel that fits your product, and why dev tools skip paid social.
3. [The maths: CAC, LTV, payback](./03-cac-ltv-payback.mdx) — the three numbers that decide whether a channel works.
4. [The shape of a Meta campaign](./04-meta-campaign-shape.mdx) — campaign/ad-set/ad, and the objective that matters most.
5. [The three audiences](./05-three-audiences.mdx) — cold, warm, and lookalike, and why seed quality wins.
6. [Your conversion tracking is lying](./06-tracking-is-lying.mdx) — why the browser Pixel under-counts.
7. [The fix: Pixel *and* Conversions API](./07-pixel-and-capi.mdx) — server-side events, deduplicated, at high match quality.
8. [Feeding it from PostHog](./08-feeding-from-posthog.mdx) — the managed CAPI path, and where Hogsend fits.
9. [Consent](./09-consent.mdx) — the part that isn't optional.
10. [Before you move on](./10-before-you-move-on.mdx) — a short checklist while it's fresh, then a quiz to check yourself.

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: Owned beats rented
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
This is the chapter that answers the original question: *how does a lifecycle tool start
driving traffic?* The answer is that lifecycle and acquisition meet at **identity** —
the more channels a person links, the cheaper they are to reach, the better your ads
perform, and the more your referrals compound. It's also the chapter where the growth
loop from Chapter 2 finally beats the funnel: an owned audience is an *input* to the next
turn, not just an output of this one.

Every ad audience is **rented.** You pay to reach those people the first time, and you
pay again every time you want to reach them after that. The moment you stop paying, the
channel goes dark. Social followings are rented too, just with a subtler landlord: the
platform's algorithm decides what fraction of "your" followers see any given post, and
that fraction has fallen for a decade across every major feed — reach you built in 2020
is not reach you have now (Chapter 7's
[law of shitty clickthroughs](https://andrewchen.com/the-law-of-shitty-clickthroughs/),
applied to organic).

An **owned** channel — a verified email address, a linked Discord or Telegram account —
is the opposite. Once someone is in it:

* **There's no algorithm tax.** A Discord post or Telegram message reaches your members
  directly; an email lands in the inbox. No boost, no bid, no feed gatekeeper deciding
  who sees it.
* **Reactivation is nearly free.** Bringing a lapsed user back through an owned channel
  costs a fraction of re-buying the ad impression.
* **It's portable.** An email list exports. A Discord guild's members are linked to
  *your* identity graph, not just to Discord. Platform policy changes hurt; they don't
  zero you.

The exact multiples you'll see quoted — "acquiring costs 5–25× more than retaining,"
"owned reactivation is 5–10× cheaper than paid" — are **directional folklore** with
loose sourcing, better treated as direction than decimals. But the **mechanism is
undeniable** and it's the load-bearing argument of this chapter: *a one-time ad spend
that captures someone into an owned channel converts a rented impression into a
reusable asset.* You pay once to acquire the contact, then reach them as often as you
like for free.

**What to take from it:**

* The reason smart teams keep pouring money into rented channels is measurability — a
  CPC is easy to report today, while the owned-audience payoff is slow and asks for
  patience and conviction. That reporting bias is what keeps the rented playbook running.
* A community only sustains itself when it would exist *without you* — built around a
  mission or topic your audience already cares about, not around your product. The moment
  the company makes itself the main character, it goes quiet.
* Distribution starts from a seed (you, your network, your existing customers); the real
  constraint isn't finding the first few people, it's making something compelling enough
  that each one wants to pass it on.

This reframes what an ad is *for*. A click that bounces is money spent and gone. A click
that ends in a verified email or a linked Discord is money spent on an asset you keep —
the same dollar, but one version evaporates and the other compounds. This is why a
lifecycle tool is the natural place for acquisition to *land*: ads bring people,
lifecycle keeps them, and the bridge between the two is the moment you capture their
identity into a channel you own.

The scale you need is smaller than you think. Kevin Kelly's
[1,000 True Fans](https://kk.org/thetechnium/1000-true-fans/) is the foundational
version of the argument: a creator (or a niche product) doesn't need millions of
renters — it needs a thousand people it can *reach directly* who care enough to buy.
An owned audience of a few thousand engaged, reachable people is a real business
foundation; a hundred thousand rented impressions is a receipt.

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: The capture mechanic: a perk for linking
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
People don't hand over their email or link their Discord out of goodwill. They do it for
a **perk.** The pattern, across every community that does this well:

> **"Link your account / verify your email → get \[a role, a perk, access, credits, a
> whitelist spot]."**

The perk is the conversion event, and the conversion *is* the identity capture. Examples
worth modelling:

* **Web3 / token-gating:** Collab.Land and Guild.xyz let a member connect a wallet, the
  bot verifies an on-chain holding, and a role unlocks gated channels or whitelist
  spots. Guild's primitive is literally **Requirements → Roles → Rewards**.
* **Quest platforms** (Zealy, Galxe, Layer3): daily actions earn XP, roles, and airdrop
  eligibility — a structured engagement loop.
* **Email-verify-for-role** (the non-web3 analog, and Hogsend's pattern): the bot links
  to a verification page; the user confirms their **email**; they get a "Verified" role
  and its perks. This is the move that produces an **email ↔ Discord** link you own —
  and several tools market it openly as list-building.

The reward works best when it maps to your product's value and lives *in the owned
channel*, so claiming it deepens the relationship rather than ending it. A perk the
user collects and walks away from buys you one email; a perk they collect *inside*
your community pulls them one step further in.

Two design considerations keep the mechanic honest:

* **The perk must be worth the link, and no more.** Over-reward and you harvest
  perk-hunters who churn the moment the reward lands (§8.7 returns to this with
  referral fraud). The best perks are things a genuine user wanted anyway: access to
  the community channels, credits in the product, early features.
* **Ask at the moment of motivation.** The link prompt converts best right after the
  user got value — the same peak-engagement timing as the referral ask (Chapter 6,
  §6.8). A cold DM asking to "verify your email for a role" is spam; the same ask as
  the doorway into a community they just decided to join is onboarding.

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: Linking Discord, with email verification
A community worth joining
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
A warning before the next code block, because this is where most "build a Discord"
advice quietly fails: **the linking mechanic captures identity; it does not create a
community.** A server with a verification gate, twenty channels, and no conversation is
a list with extra steps — and everyone who joins can *see* it's dead, which a mailing
list at least hides.

The practitioners' consensus (Jono Bacon, who wrote the standard books on this, is the
clearest voice) compresses to:

* **Start with a mission, not a server.** People join a movement or a topic, not your
  product's support channel. Frame the community around the thing your users are trying
  to *become* — better at growth, better at shipping — with your product as the venue.
* **Fewer channels than feels right.** Three active channels beat twenty empty ones.
  Conversation density is the product; split channels only when volume forces it.
* **Rituals create the habit.** A weekly show-and-tell, a changelog thread, an office
  hour — recurring, hosted moments give members a reason to come back on a schedule
  (the community version of Chapter 9's operating cadence).
* **The first 100 members are hand-recruited.** Like Chapter 7's do-things-that-don't-
  scale: personally invite, personally welcome, personally introduce members to each
  other until conversation is self-sustaining.

**What to take from it:**

* A community isn't a platform, it's a belief system — "platforms are where people talk;
  movements are why they care." Define your creed (the principles your audience already
  holds), your non-believers (who does it the wrong way), and your rituals (repeatable
  actions) *before* you open any server.
* Don't start with a platform — start with an event. Gather even ten people into a live
  session to share and learn, *then* give them the lightest possible digital home on
  whatever platform they already use (Discord, Slack, even a mailing list).
* Engagement is orchestrated, not automatic: seed two or three easy conversation-starters
  a week, then do the "awkward small talk" — follow up, make introductions, broker
  connections — and measure DAU/MAU as the heartbeat.

**What to take from it:**

* Foundation first: state a clear purpose in the welcome channel, build a real onboarding
  experience (start-here channel, FAQ, a "how did you find us?" prompt), and keep channels
  few — merge or archive underused ones so conversation stays dense.
* Drive engagement with recurring rituals: scheduled events with reminders, polls for
  feedback, celebrating member milestones, icebreakers, and channels for user-generated
  content and contests.
* Let bots do the heavy lifting: welcome bots, automated moderation, reaction-roles for
  self-service interests, gamified XP/leaderboards, and stat bots — then share the metrics
  back to the community to build momentum.

The measurement lens from this course applies here too: instrument the community like
a product. `member.joined`, first message, reactions given/received — Hogsend's
connector events — give you an activation funnel for the community itself (what
fraction of joiners post within a week?) and feed the engagement-role journeys above.
[Discord's own community resources](https://discord.com/community) and the
[CMX community-building guide](https://www.cmxhub.com/reports/the-cmx-guide-to-building-a-community)
are the fuller playbooks.

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: Linking Telegram
The identity graph
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
Every link event writes an **edge** between two ways of reaching the same person:

```
email ↔ Discord ID ↔ Telegram ID ↔ wallet ↔ anonymous web id ↔ PostHog person
```

Collect enough edges and you have an **identity graph**: one unified profile that spans
devices, sessions, and channels. The payoffs stack:

* **Retroactive stitching.** When an anonymous visitor finally gives an email or links
  Discord, all their prior touchpoints attach to the now-known profile — so attribution
  works on data *you own*, independent of cookies.
* **Channel choice on reactivation.** A lapsed user isn't reachable only by an ad you
  must re-buy — you can email them, ping them in Discord, or DM them on Telegram. The
  denser the graph, the more ways back in, and the lower your cost to re-reach anyone.
* **Better ads, legally.** Those verified, consented emails are exactly the high-EMQ
  custom-audience and lookalike-seed inputs from Chapter 7. The graph you build here is
  the fuel the ad machine there runs on.

There's a strategic layer on top of the operational one. Chris Dixon's
[come for the tool, stay for the network](https://cdixon.org/2015/01/31/come-for-the-tool-stay-for-the-network/)
describes the classic ladder: a single-player tool earns the user, and the network they
join is what keeps them (and keeps competitors out). A linked community sits exactly on
that ladder — the product is the tool; the community and its graph are the network. And
a16z's [Hidden Networks](https://a16z.com/hidden-networks-network-effects-that-dont-look-like-network-effects/)
makes the subtler point that defensible networks often don't look like social networks —
an identity graph connecting your users' channels, reachable by you and no competitor,
is precisely such a hidden network. Features can be copied in a quarter; a dense,
consented identity graph has to be earned edge by edge.

Hogsend builds this graph on its **own** identity model (it stitches email ↔ channel ↔
anonymous), and its connectors emit the engagement events — `discord.linked`,
`reaction_added`, `member.joined`, `link.clicked` — that both trigger journeys *and* add
edges. The graph is the hub of the flywheel; every channel you link spins it faster. And
because it's *yours* — not a rented list inside someone else's platform — it's the
compounding asset a growth team is actually building over time. Features come and go;
the identity graph only gets denser.

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: The referral loop
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
Referrals are where the owned graph pays off most, because **a referral program only
works if you can attribute it** — and attribution is exactly what the graph gives you.
The prize is real:
[Lenny Rachitsky's survey of referral programs](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/this-week-16-building-a-referrals)
found they commonly drive **15–50% of new-user growth** at companies that get them
right — and, in the same breath, that most programs see **under 10% of users ever
refer anyone**. Both numbers matter: the upside is large, and the median program
underdelivers because it was bolted on rather than designed.

### Incentive structures that work

* **Double-sided (give-get).** Reward *both* the referrer and the new user. It removes
  the "I'm asking a favour" friction. **Dropbox** is the canonical case: 500 MB of
  storage to *each side* per referral, capped at 16 GB. The reward was the product's own
  core value, the ask was the last step of onboarding, and it drove **~3,900% growth in
  15 months.** (The backstory: paid ads were costing Dropbox far more than a customer was
  worth, so a product-value referral was dramatically cheaper.)
* **Milestone / tiered.** An escalating ladder of rewards — **Morning Brew**'s newsletter
  referral program (stickers → shirt → bigger swag → trip to HQ) is the model, with a
  leaderboard for status. Near-zero marginal cost per reward; great for community.
* **Status / access.** For pre-launch and web3/games, the reward is *earlier access*, a
  *whitelist spot*, or an *exclusive role* — scarcity instead of cash. **Robinhood**'s
  skip-the-line waitlist is the classic.

The design principles that run across all of them: sharing that costs nothing
(prewritten copy, a one-click link), rewards on both sides, a reward aligned to product
value, visible progress (a dashboard or leaderboard), and **an ask timed to peak
engagement** — right after the aha moment, which is the referral journey from Chapter
6, §6.8.

The two levers a loop actually turns on are *invites sent per user* and *the share of
invites that convert* — their product is the k-factor. Below 1 a loop doesn't sustain
itself, but it still quietly multiplies every other channel; at or above 1 you have true
organic virality. See what your assumptions imply before you build:

### The honest failure mode: referred users can be your worst users

Before settling on a reward, it's worth sitting with Andrew Chen's
[counterpoint](https://andrewchen.com/worst-users-referral-trials-coupons-gamification/):
users acquired through heavy incentives — referral bounties, coupons, gamified
rewards — are often the *lowest-quality* cohort you acquire, because the incentive
(not the product) did the selecting. The symptoms: referred users who never activate,
reward-farmers inviting throwaway accounts, and a referral "channel" whose cohort
retention embarrasses every other source.

The defences are all things this course already gave you:

* **Cohorting the referred users** (Chapter 4) and comparing their retention against
  other channels — a referral program is judged on retained users, not invites sent.
* **Rewarding verified behaviour, not signups.** When the referee has to verify email,
  link an account, or hit a real activation milestone before the reward fires, farmers
  lose interest. This is exactly the anti-fraud power the identity graph gives you
  (below).
* **A reward aligned to product value** (Dropbox's storage, not cash), so the people
  the incentive attracts are people who wanted the product anyway.

### Why the owned graph makes attribution work

Cookie-based referral tracking breaks on the exact journey referrals take: a friend
shares a link, the recipient clicks on their phone, then signs up on a laptop weeks
later. Last-click misses it; the referrer goes uncredited; the program looks like it
doesn't work and gets cut.

An owned identity graph fixes this:

* First-party referral codes/links are tracked **independent of third-party cookies**,
  so you capture the conversion even as browsers tighten.
* The graph **resolves the new user across devices** and **back-credits the referrer**
  when the anonymous visitor finally identifies (gives email / links Discord).
* You can enforce **anti-fraud rules** — the referee must verify their email or link an
  account or hit a real milestone before the reward fires — because the *whole graph is
  yours*. A Discord-linked referral resolves to a real verified person, not a cookie.

And the natural community fit closes the loop: **"X verified referrals → unlock a Discord
role"** puts the reward *in the owned channel*, which densifies the graph further. The
reward for growing your audience is itself a deeper hook into your audience.

Hogsend ships a referral v1 on exactly this shape: `visited → converted → credited`, two
journeys plus a Discord role granted at a milestone — all on its own identity graph, no
cookies required.

### Build vs buy: the referral tooling landscape

You don't have to build referral attribution from scratch, and a growth team should know
the off-the-shelf options:

* **Rewardful / GrowSurf / Referral Rock / Viral Loops** — hosted referral SaaS, fast to
  stand up, with dashboards and reward logic out of the box. **Buy when** you want a
  program live this week and a simple link-based attribution is enough.
* **PartnerStack / FirstPromoter** — affiliate/partner-program flavoured, for paid
  referrers and influencers rather than user-to-user.
* **Build it on your own graph (Hogsend's approach)** — **worth it when** cross-device
  and cross-channel attribution matters, when the reward lives in your product or
  community (a role, credits, access), and when you want anti-fraud tied to real verified
  identity. The hosted tools mostly rely on cookies/links and can't resolve the
  phone-click → laptop-signup gap; your graph can.

The honest split: a hosted tool is the right first move for a simple cash/credit referral
on a single channel. The owned-graph build wins precisely when referrals are
cross-device, community-rewarded, or fraud-prone — which is most of the interesting cases
for the products this course is written for.

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: Other ways to own an audience
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
Discord/Telegram-on-your-own-infra is this course's emphasis because it builds the graph
you own outright. But "owned audience" is a spectrum, and the alternatives are worth
naming:

* **Newsletter platforms (beehiiv, ConvertKit, Substack).** A hosted email list is a
  genuinely owned audience in the sense that matters most — you can export it and reach it
  without an algorithm. beehiiv even has a built-in referral program. **Use one when**
  email is your whole channel and you don't need the behavioural-journey or identity-graph
  depth. The trade-off: your list and its data live in their platform, and you don't get
  the cross-channel graph.
* **Community platforms (Circle, Slack, Discord, Geneva).** Circle and Slack are
  higher-polish, more B2B-feeling; Discord is the default for consumer/gaming/web3 and the
  one with the richest bot ecosystem for the link-for-perk mechanic. Choose by where your
  audience already is.
* **The fully-hosted CRM/audience suites (HubSpot, Customer.io audiences).** Convenient,
  and they own the data model. Fine if you've decided to rent the infrastructure.
