The principle that ranks them: **the more of the identity graph you own outright, the more
the audience compounds and the less you're exposed to a platform's pricing or policy
changes.** A hosted newsletter is a fine, real owned audience; an identity graph on your
own infrastructure is the maximal version. The rung worth picking is the one that
matches how central owned growth is to your business.

### For your kind of startup

* **Consumer / gaming / web3** — Discord is the owned channel; the link-for-a-role
  mechanic is both activation and acquisition. This is your main chapter.
* **Dev tool** — a mix: a high-quality newsletter (changelog/deep-dives) plus a Discord
  or Slack for support and community. The docs-to-Discord path is your loop.
* **B2B SaaS** — email list + maybe a Slack community for customers; referrals are often
  partner/affiliate-shaped (PartnerStack territory) rather than viral.
* **E-commerce** — email + SMS list is the owned audience; post-purchase referral
  (give-get discount) is the canonical loop, and a hosted tool is usually enough.

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: The full funnel, assembled
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
Putting Chapters 7 and 8 together, this is the complete path — and every stage captures
an identity edge:

| Stage | Goal | What's captured |
|---|---|---|
| **Ad / content** | Cold reach | `fbclid` / UTM on the landing page |
| **Landing** | Intent | anon id stitched to the click |
| **Email capture + verify** | Own the contact | **email** (consented) |
| **Discord/Telegram link (perk)** | Densify the graph | **email ↔ Discord/Telegram** |
| **Lifecycle journeys** | Activate & retain | engagement events on your own spine |
| **Referral loop** | Compound | referrer ↔ referee edge, often a Discord role |

The feedback loops that make it a flywheel rather than a funnel — the
[growth-loops model](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-loops) from Chapter 2, finally
built:

* Verified emails → **custom audiences + lookalike seeds** → cheaper, better-matched ads
  (Chapter 7).
* Server-side deep conversions → **smarter Meta optimisation** (Chapter 7).
* Owned channels → **near-free reactivation** (this chapter).
* Referrals → **growth that lowers blended CAC over time** (this chapter).

The identity graph sits at the hub. Every turn of the loop adds edges, and every edge
makes the next turn cheaper. That's the difference between a funnel you keep refilling and
a loop that compounds.

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
A few things worth doing while this chapter is fresh:

1. **Choose the perk** that's worth linking for (a role, access, credits, a whitelist).
   The sweet spot: valuable enough to convert, not so valuable it attracts farmers —
   §8.2's two design considerations are the test.
2. **Stand up the Discord and/or Telegram connector** with the `/link` email-verify
   flow — this is the moment your list starts becoming a graph.
3. **The `discord-linked` / `telegram-linked` journey** grants the perk and welcomes
   people across both channels; it's the code from §8.3, nearly verbatim.
4. **Give the community a reason to exist beyond the perk**: a mission statement, three
   channels, one weekly ritual (§8.4). The first members are worth recruiting by hand —
   conversation density is what everyone who joins next will judge you on.
5. **Place the referral ask at your aha milestone** (Chapter 6, §6.8) with verified
   referrals rewarded by a role — and it's worth putting "referred-cohort retention" on
   the dashboard before celebrating the invite count.
6. **Sync your verified, consented emails** as a Meta custom audience and lookalike
   seed (Chapter 7) — this is where the two chapters' machines connect.

That's the whole machine. Chapter 9 turns running it into a weekly rhythm, and Chapter
10 sequences everything you've built into a 30/60/90/180-day plan.

***

***

## Go deeper

* [Kevin Kelly — 1,000 True Fans](https://kk.org/thetechnium/1000-true-fans/) — the foundational owned-audience thesis: direct reach to a small true-fan base beats large rented reach.
* [Reforge — Growth Loops are the new funnels](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-loops) — the loop model this chapter's flywheel implements.
* [Lenny Rachitsky — Building a referrals program](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/this-week-16-building-a-referrals) — mechanics and benchmarks: 15–50% of growth when done right, under 10% of users typically refer.
* [Andrew Chen — Worst users come from referral programs](https://andrewchen.com/worst-users-referral-trials-coupons-gamification/) — the essential counterpoint; read before setting the reward.
* [Chris Dixon — Come for the tool, stay for the network](https://cdixon.org/2015/01/31/come-for-the-tool-stay-for-the-network/) — the strategic ladder from single-player product to network moat.
* [a16z — Hidden Networks](https://a16z.com/hidden-networks-network-effects-that-dont-look-like-network-effects/) — why an identity graph is a network effect that doesn't look like one.
* [Discord — Community resources](https://discord.com/community) — the primary-source playbook for server structure, onboarding, and moderation.
* [CMX — Guide to Building a Community](https://www.cmxhub.com/reports/the-cmx-guide-to-building-a-community) — the fuller community-led-growth framework behind §8.4.
* [Guild.xyz](https://help.guild.xyz/en/articles/6934383-introduction-to-guild) and [Collab.Land](https://docs.collab.land/help-docs/key-features/token-gate-communities/) — the token-gating implementations of the perk-for-linking mechanic.
* [Telegram — deep-link docs](https://core.telegram.org/api/links) — the `?start=` primitive Telegram referrals ride on.

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: 8 — The owned-audience flywheel
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
> **In this chapter:** why an owned channel beats a rented ad audience, the
> "link-your-account-for-a-perk" mechanic that captures identity, how email
> verification and Discord/Telegram linking build an identity graph, what actually
> keeps a community alive once you've built one, how the graph makes referral loops
> work (and their honest failure modes), and the off-the-shelf tools you could use
> instead. Code mirrors Hogsend's real connectors.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into ten short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. Where a piece has a video, the pattern is the same each time: read the
one-line reason it's worth your time, watch it (or read the transcript underneath), then
read the short digest so the point sticks. Work through them in order, or jump to what you
need:

1. [Owned beats rented](./01-owned-beats-rented.mdx) — a rented impression evaporates; a captured contact compounds.
2. [The capture mechanic: a perk for linking](./02-perk-for-linking.mdx) — the conversion event *is* the identity capture.
3. [Linking Discord, with email verification](./03-linking-discord.mdx) — the typed address is never trusted until the email is clicked.
4. [A community worth joining](./04-community-worth-joining.mdx) — the linking mechanic captures identity; it doesn't create a community.
5. [Linking Telegram](./05-linking-telegram.mdx) — no native email, so the same proof-by-clicking flow binds the edge.
6. [The identity graph](./06-identity-graph.mdx) — every link event writes an edge, and the edges compound.
7. [The referral loop](./07-referral-loop.mdx) — where the owned graph pays off most, plus its honest failure mode.
8. [Other ways to own an audience](./08-other-ways-to-own.mdx) — the spectrum from a hosted newsletter to your own graph.
9. [The full funnel, assembled](./09-full-funnel.mdx) — Chapters 7 and 8 joined into one identity-capturing path.
10. [Before you move on](./10-before-you-move-on.mdx) — six things to do while it's fresh, then a quiz to check yourself.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: The whole thing, in one loop
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
Everything in this course is one loop with the **identity graph** at its hub:

```
        ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │                                                         │
   drive traffic (Ch 7)                                           │
   ads · content · community                                      │
        │                                                         │
        ▼                                                         │
   capture identity (Ch 8)          measure everything (Ch 1–4)   │
   email verify · link Discord  ───► PostHog: funnels, retention  │
   /Telegram · perks for linking     the daily dashboard          │
        │                                  │                      │
        ▼                                  ▼                      │
   ┌─────────── IDENTITY GRAPH ───────────┐                       │
   │ email ↔ Discord ↔ Telegram ↔ anon    │                       │
   └──────────────────┬───────────────────┘                      │
                      │                                           │
                      ▼                                           │
   keep them (Ch 5–6)                                             │
   lifecycle journeys in Hogsend ──► fire conversions + audiences ┘
   welcome · nudge · win-back ·       back to the ads (Ch 7)
   dunning · referral                 → referrals lower CAC (Ch 8)
```

Read it as a sentence: **you measure so you can see the leaks; you keep users with
lifecycle journeys so the bucket holds; then you drive traffic and capture every visitor
into an audience you own; that audience feeds better ads and a referral loop, which
lowers your cost of growth — and the whole thing is measurable because every step fires
an event.**

The reason the order is *measure → keep → grow* and not the reverse is the leaky bucket
(Chapter 2). Acquisition is the last lever because it's the one that wastes the most
money when the bucket leaks and pays back the most when it doesn't. That single ordering
decision, agreed by the team back in Chapter 0, is the most valuable thing in the course.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: Who does what
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
A quick reference for the division of labour the course is built on:

| Job | Tool | Chapters |
|---|---|---|
| Decide what to measure (AARRR, North Star) and own the order | You | 0, 2 |
| Collect events, identify people | PostHog | 1, 3 |
| See the funnel, retention, leaks | PostHog | 2, 4 |
| Run lifecycle journeys (email, Discord, Telegram) | Hogsend | 5, 6, 8 |
| Drive paid traffic | Meta ads (or the right channel) | 7 |
| Track conversions accurately | Pixel + CAPI, via PostHog | 7 |
| Capture & link identity, referrals | Hogsend connectors | 8 |

**PostHog detects. Hogsend acts. You decide.** Held onto, that split keeps the system
simple no matter how much you add to it.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: The build order at a glance
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
The sequence is the course's spine, compressed to four gates:

1. **Day 0 — commit.** Naming the growth owner, writing the activation sentence,
   agreeing retention-before-acquisition as a team (Chapter 0).
2. **Days 1–30 — Measure.** PostHog live, a tracking plan, the activation event, the
   daily dashboard. The gate: you can say where the funnel leaks, with numbers
   (Chapters 1–4).
3. **Days 31–60 — Keep.** Welcome, dunning, win-back journeys — with a holdout. The
   gate: lift you've measured rather than sensed (Chapters 5–6).
4. **Days 61–180 — Grow, then compound.** Open the tap, capture identity into owned
   channels, then let the whole thing become an experimentation program (Chapters 7–8,
   and the experimentation program below).

That's the summary; **Chapter 10 is the full plan** — each phase with its interactive
checklist, exit criteria, realistic targets, and the anti-patterns that blow it up.
This chapter is about what keeps the machine alive *after* it's built: the cadence and
the experimentation culture.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: The operating cadence
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
Once it's built, it runs on a rhythm, not on heroics. This is the ritual that makes you
a growth *team* rather than a team that did a growth project once (Chapter 0). The
principle — [Reforge calls it your operating
cadence](https://www.reforge.com/blog/operating-cadence) — is that the *rhythm* of
looking is itself a designed system: decide in advance what gets read daily, weekly,
and monthly, and by whom, so attention stops depending on anyone's memory.

**Daily (2 minutes).** The daily job is *noticing*, not analysing: a glance at the
Slack alerts, the activation funnel, and the active-users trend — and the dashboard
itself only if an alert fired. If the daily glance regularly takes more than five
minutes, that's usually a sign the alerts need tuning, not that you need more
discipline.

**Weekly (30 minutes, the growth review).** The one meeting this course asks you to
hold, and it has a fixed agenda — a compressed version of the weekly growth meeting
[Brian Balfour has documented](https://brianbalfour.com/essays/growth-meeting) from
running growth at HubSpot and Reforge:

1. **Metrics read (10 min).** The retention curve and lifecycle chart — is growth real
   or churn-and-replace? North Star vs last week. Anomalies get noted and assigned an
   owner rather than debugged live; group debugging is how a 30-minute meeting becomes
   a 2-hour one.
2. **Journeys and spend (10 min).** Each live journey's send volume and holdout lift;
   ad performance — cost per conversion, EMQ, which audiences are working. This is
   where kill-or-scale decisions get made, out loud.
3. **Experiments (10 min).** What finished (result and learning, a sentence each),
   what launches next from the top of the backlog (the experimentation program), and
   what's blocked.

The discipline that makes the meeting work: it reviews **learnings and decisions**, not
status. "The welcome-nudge holdout shows +4 points activation, we're keeping it and
removing the day-5 email" is a growth review; "we sent 3,400 emails this week" is
theatre.

Two habits tend to close the weekly loop well. Ending the week with **3–5 session
replays** of the biggest drop-off gives you the qualitative *why* behind the
quantitative *where* (Chapter 4). And **writing the week's one learning down**
somewhere permanent is cheaper than it looks — a learning that lives in one person's
head is one the team usually pays to re-discover six months later.

**Monthly (an hour).** Zoom out to the questions that are too slow to see weekly:

* The **activation metric** — is it still the best predictor of retention? Chapter 2's
  correlation-then-experiment method is there whenever you're unsure.
* **Incremental lift** from the lifecycle program (global holdout) — the number that
  justifies the whole Keep phase to anyone who asks.
* **Blended CAC** and the referral loop's contribution. Is growth getting cheaper as
  the owned audience grows? That's Chapter 8's flywheel, showing up in the finances.
* **Pruning.** Journeys with no lift, events no one queries, ad sets that don't
  convert — a growth system accretes cruft exactly like a codebase, and a monthly
  prune keeps it legible.

The cadence is the point. A growth system that's checked on a rhythm survives a busy
quarter; one that depends on someone remembering to look does not.

**What to take from it:**

* Culture change is an artifact, not a pep talk: write an operating manual and a short
  list of team values — "we experiment to find what works" first — and *launch* it
  internally like a feature so the team knows the rhythm is real.
* The cadence is where it lives or dies: reinforce it in the weekly planning meeting
  and 1:1s ("where did you get to question how we've always done things this week?"),
  so it's a standing question, not a one-time talk.
* Lead by dogfooding failure: a leader who stands up and shares an experiment where
  their own hypothesis was flat wrong gives the team permission to run tests that don't
  pan out — which is the whole point of tempo.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: The experimentation program
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
The cadence keeps the machine observed; experiments are how it improves. The difference
between a team that occasionally A/B tests and a team with an experimentation *program*
is three artifacts:

**A scored backlog.** Every growth idea — a new journey, a pricing page change, an
onboarding step removed — goes into one list as a **hypothesis** rather than a task:
*"if we \[change], \[metric] will move \[direction] because \[reasoning]."* Scoring each on
**[ICE](https://www.productplan.com/glossary/ice-scoring-model)** — impact, confidence,
ease, each 1–10 — lets the scores order the list.
([PIE](https://conversion.com/framework/pie-framework/) — potential, importance, ease —
is the same idea with different letters; either works, and the only real mistake is
debating frameworks instead of running tests.) The score's job isn't precision — it's
letting the *"this would be huge"* claim meet *"how confident are we, actually?"*, and
giving the weekly meeting a top of the list to pull from instead of the loudest idea
in the room.

**A steady tempo.** Sean Ellis — the man who coined both "growth hacking" and the
North Star framing this course uses — argues the compounding asset is your
**velocity of tests**: more shots on goal means faster learning, and the learning is
what compounds even when individual tests fail.

**What to take from it:**

* The best "growth hacks" aren't tricks — they're finding where people get confused in
  the product today and making it simpler. It starts with qualitative analysis: why do
  your must-have users actually love the product? You can't improve what you don't
  understand.
* Volume of experiments is the lever, echoing Bezos's line that Amazon's success is a
  function of how many experiments it runs per day, month, and year — nobody reliably
  knows in advance which idea will work, so the only way to find the big win is to test
  a lot of things.
* The Gretzky framing: you miss every shot you don't take. Running three-to-five tests
  a week beats obsessing over one "perfect" test, because any single test might be the
  outsized winner and you can't tell which in advance.

Reforge's [experiment management
system](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-experiment-management-system) puts a
number on the same idea — a standard template plus a visible backlog roughly tripled
one team's testing velocity — and [First Round's growth-sprint
process](https://review.firstround.com/the-secret-to-running-effective-growth-sprints-follow-this-process-to-learn-faster/)
wraps it in a sprint so every experiment ends in a written learning. For a startup,
"high tempo" might mean one experiment a week. That's fine. One a week, every week,
with results written down, beats a burst of ten followed by a quarter of nothing.

**Honest rules.** Tempo without discipline manufactures false wins, so the program
needs three:

* **Not everything deserves an experiment.** Obvious fixes, reversible changes, and
  changes too small to measure can just ship —
  [Lenny's guide to when *not* to test](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/not-running-an-experiment)
  is a good checklist. Experiments earn their overhead on decisions that are expensive
  to get wrong.
* **Sample size decides what you can learn.** At startup traffic, most tests can't
  detect small effects — which is why big swings (whole journeys, whole onboarding
  flows) tend to beat button colours, and why "no detectable difference" is an honest,
  common result rather than a failure.
  [Kohavi and Lenny's A/B testing guide](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/the-ultimate-guide-to-ab-testing)
  covers the traps — peeking, underpowered wins, p-hacking by another name.
* **The losers are worth writing down too.** A result only really counts once it's
  recorded — hypothesis, what happened, what you'd try differently. The write-up
  takes thirty seconds; re-running a forgotten failed experiment takes a month.

Where does the *content* of the backlog come from? The machine you built:
the funnel's biggest drop-off (Chapter 4), the journey with no lift (Chapter 5), the
channel with decent volume but poor activation (Chapter 7), the replay where users rage-
click (Chapter 4). If the backlog feels empty, the dashboard is being read too politely.
[YC's growth curriculum](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6k-growth-for-startups)
makes the same loop explicit at whole-company scale: pick the one metric, find the
constraint, test against it, repeat.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: The patterns that undo it
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
Every team that builds this system brushes against the same failure modes. It's worth
knowing them by name — each one links back to the chapter with the escape route:

* **Scaling acquisition into a leaky bucket** (Ch 2). The most expensive of the set —
  retention work first is the escape.
* **Chasing someone else's magic number** (Ch 2). The famous activation numbers are
  rally cries, not physics; the correlation-then-experiment method finds *yours*.
* **Building event names dynamically** (Ch 3). One of the quickest ways to ruin an
  analytics setup — one fixed event with variables in properties keeps it queryable.
* **Installing analytics and never looking** (Ch 0, Ch 4). The dashboard graveyard.
  Measurement without a ritual and without action ends up as theatre.
* **Experiment theatre** (the experimentation program). Underpowered tests
  "validating" decisions already made, wins nobody could reproduce, losers never
  written down. Tempo counts learnings, not launches.
* **Trusting the Pixel alone** (Ch 7). It under-counts by a third or more; Pixel *and*
  CAPI, deduped, fed from where you have identity, is the reliable shape.
* **Treating server-side as a consent loophole** (Ch 7). Hashed email is pseudonymous,
  not anonymous — consent still needs capturing.
* **Measuring opens instead of lift** (Ch 5). Opens don't prove causation; holdouts do.
* **Renting your whole audience** (Ch 8). Identity captured into owned channels is
  what saves you from paying to reach the same person twice.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: You're a growth team now
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
Come back to where the course started. The tools were never the hard part — you could
install PostHog, Hogsend, and a pixel in an afternoon. The thing that actually changed,
if you did the work, is how your team *operates*:

* You **measure honestly** — decisions cite the funnel, not the loudest opinion, and
  "activated" means a specific event, not a vibe.
* You **act automatically** — the messages that pull users back to value are journeys in
  your repo, triggered by behaviour, code-reviewed like the rest of the product, and
  proven with holdouts.
* You **own your audience** — every dollar of acquisition lands in a channel you can reach
  again for free, on an identity graph that only gets denser.
* You **learn on a rhythm** — a weekly cadence, a scored backlog, a steady tempo of
  experiments whose results get written down, winners and losers alike.

That's the whole definition of a product-led growth team from Chapter 0, and you'll
notice none of it required a growth department. It required one owner, one loop, and a
rhythm. A founder who can read a retention curve and ship a journey *is* the growth team —
and the system you've built is what lets a team that small operate like one many times its
size.

It also doesn't matter what your startup is. The tactics flexed by type all the way
through — activation means a first match for a marketplace, a first deploy for a dev tool,
a second purchase for e-commerce, a linked Discord for a community — but the operating
model never changed: instrument, measure, act, own, repeat. That portability is the whole
point. Growth stops being a thing you hope works and becomes a system you run.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
Three things worth doing while this chapter is fresh:

1. **The weekly growth review** — booking it now, 30 minutes recurring with the
   operating-cadence agenda, is probably the highest-leverage calendar invite in the
   course. A rhythm that exists in the calendar survives; one that exists as an
   intention doesn't.
2. **The experiment backlog** — one shared doc, the hypothesis format from the
   experimentation program, ICE scores. Your dashboard's three worst numbers are the
   natural first entries.
3. **Chapter 10** — the full 30/60/90/180-day plan with interactive checklists. It's
   where all of this becomes a sequence with dates on it.

***

***

## Go deeper

* [Reforge — Rethinking your operating cadence](https://www.reforge.com/blog/operating-cadence) — the daily/weekly/monthly rhythm as a designed system, not a habit.
* [Brian Balfour — How to run a weekly growth meeting](https://brianbalfour.com/essays/growth-meeting) — the full agenda the operating cadence compresses, from the person who ran it at HubSpot.
* [Reforge — The growth experiment management system](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-experiment-management-system) — backlog + template + velocity metrics; the "tripled our testing velocity" case.
* [First Round Review — Running effective growth sprints](https://review.firstround.com/the-secret-to-running-effective-growth-sprints-follow-this-process-to-learn-faster/) — wrapping experiments in a sprint so each one ends in a written learning.
* [SaaStr — Sean Ellis on building a company-wide growth culture](https://www.saastr.com/sean-ellis-founder-ceo-of-growthhackers-building-a-company-wide-growth-culture-video-transcript/) — high-tempo testing and the growth-master role, in the primary voice.
* [Lenny Rachitsky — When NOT to run an experiment](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/not-running-an-experiment) — the discipline half of tempo.
* [Lenny Rachitsky & Ronny Kohavi — The ultimate guide to A/B testing](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/the-ultimate-guide-to-ab-testing) — trustworthy results at honest sample sizes.
* [ProductPlan — ICE scoring](https://www.productplan.com/glossary/ice-scoring-model) and [Conversion.com — PIE](https://conversion.com/framework/pie-framework/) — the two standard backlog-scoring schemes; pick one.
* [YC — Growth for startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6k-growth-for-startups) — the whole loop at company scale, from Airbnb's former growth lead.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: 9 — Putting it all together
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
> **In this chapter:** the end-to-end system as a single loop, the daily/weekly/monthly
> rhythm that turns it from a project into an operation, how to run experiments at a
> steady tempo without fooling yourself, the patterns that tend to undo it — and a
> return to the question the course opened with: what it takes to run your startup as a
> product-led growth team. The sequenced build plan itself lives in Chapter 10.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into eight short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. Two carry a short video: read the one-line reason it's worth your
time, watch it (or read the transcript underneath), then read the digest so the point
sticks. Work through them in order, or jump to what you need:

1. [The whole thing, in one loop](./01-one-loop.mdx) — the entire course as a single diagram.
2. [Who does what](./02-who-does-what.mdx) — PostHog detects, Hogsend acts, you decide.
3. [The build order at a glance](./03-build-order.mdx) — four gates, and why the order holds.
4. [The operating cadence](./04-operating-cadence.mdx) — the daily/weekly/monthly rhythm.
5. [The experimentation program](./05-experimentation-program.mdx) — scored backlog, steady tempo, honest rules.
6. [The patterns that undo it](./06-patterns-that-undo-it.mdx) — the failure modes, named.
7. [You're a growth team now](./07-youre-a-growth-team.mdx) — what actually changed.
8. [Before you move on](./08-before-you-move-on.mdx) — three things to do, then the quiz.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: How to use this plan
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
Everything before this chapter taught the machine; this chapter sequences the build. A
few rules about how to read it:

* **The phases are gates, not dates.** "30/60/90/180" assumes roughly a founder-day per
  week of focused growth work. With more time you can compress; with less, stretch —
  the one thing worth protecting is the *order*, because each phase's exit criteria
  are the entry requirements for the next. Shipping ads (phase 3) before retention
  work (phase 2) is the leaky-bucket pattern from Chapter 2, now with a budget
  attached.
* **The plan is stage-agnostic, the targets aren't.** A pre-launch product runs the same
  sequence but treats phase 1 as "instrument before launch" and phase 3 as launch
  channels rather than paid scaling. Where a number matters, this chapter says what it
  gates rather than pretending one benchmark fits every product — Chapter 2's warning
  about chasing someone else's magic number applies to plans too.
* **This is the same first-90-days shape growth operators run when they land in a new
  role** — learn the data first, find early wins in activation/retention, only then
  scale acquisition ([Reforge's first-90-days
  guide](https://www.reforge.com/guides/succeed-in-your-new-growth-leadership-role),
  [Delivering Value's head-of-growth
  plan](https://deliveringvalue.co/growth-essays/head-of-growth)). You're running it on
  your own company.

**What to take from it:**

* The first month is mostly *looking*: build a learning roadmap — product depth first
  (sign up as a real user with a plus-address, hit the paywalls), then how the team and
  tools actually work — before you commit to projects. It's the same "measure before you
  move" instinct as the Measure phase below.
* Pick work in the "Goldilocks zone": important enough to move a metric *and* squarely in
  your existing skill set, so you bank quick wins and trust before the harder bets — the
  operator's version of "retention before acquisition".
* Guard your capacity out loud — cap the work in progress, name your quick-wins-vs-experiments
  split — because the two fastest ways to derail a plan are picking the wrong projects and
  taking on too much.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: Day 0 — commit (one hour, no code)
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
Chapter 0's commitments, now as a checklist. They're best done in one sitting with the
whole team present — an hour, and it tends to prevent the two most expensive patterns
in the course: nobody owning the loop, and acquisition before retention.

The last two matter more than they look. A recurring 30-minute meeting is the smallest
possible container for a growth *ritual* — the thing Chapter 9 argues separates a
growth team from a team that did a growth project once. And putting growth work in the
same backlog as product work is what makes "growth is an engineering discipline"
(Chapter 0) real rather than aspirational.

Two of those commitments are *writing* commitments, so there's space for them right
here. Both save to [your workbook](/workbook) alongside everything else you put down
in the course, linked back to the lesson it came from.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: Days 1–30 — Measure
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
**Goal: see the truth about your funnel.** Nothing in this phase grows the business
directly. All of it determines whether the next five months of work target the real
leak or a guessed one.

Week by week, the shape most teams follow:

| Week | Focus | Chapters |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | PostHog live: SDK installed, `identify`/`reset` wired, starter events firing | 1, 3 |
| 2 | Tracking plan written; activation event defined; server-side revenue events | 2, 3 |
| 3 | Reverse proxy; data QA — watch replays, check identities merge correctly | 3 |
| 4 | The daily dashboard built and pinned; alerts to Slack; first honest read | 4 |

**Exit criteria.** You can open the dashboard and say, with numbers: *where* in the
funnel you lose the most people, *what* your week-4 retention roughly is, and *which*
phase of product-market fit you're in (Chapter 4, §4.3). If those three sentences
aren't sayable yet, that's the signal to stay in this phase a little longer —
[YC's analytics-for-startups
guidance](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8H-analytics-for-startups) is blunt that
most startups' first analytics problem is not volume of charts but trusting what the
few important ones say.

**The pattern to watch for here** is instrumenting forever. Thirty days is enough to
measure a funnel honestly; it is not enough to build a data platform — a schema
registry in week three is usually growth work quietly traded for tooling work.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: Days 31–60 — Keep
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
**Goal: plug the biggest leaks before spending a cent on traffic.** This is the phase
with the highest return per hour in the whole plan — the users it saves are users you
already paid (in money or effort) to acquire.

Journeys are best built in the order of expected impact, which for almost every
product looks like:

1. **Welcome + onboarding nudge** — the gap between signup and activation is nearly
   always the biggest leak (Chapter 5). One journey: welcome on signup, a nudge at
   +48h if the activation event hasn't fired.
2. **Dunning** — if you charge money, failed payments are pure, recoverable churn
   (Chapter 6). This tends to be the fastest revenue win in the whole course.
3. **Win-back** — dormant-user re-engagement, triggered by an inactivity window, with
   something concrete to come back to.

**Exit criteria.** Either your activation or early-retention number has visibly moved,
or — more honestly — your **holdout comparison** shows the journeys lifting it
(Chapter 5's incrementality argument: opens and clicks are activity, lift is proof).
The bucket holds water well enough that pouring into it isn't waste.

**The pattern to watch for** is building all eight journeys you can imagine instead of
the three that matter — volume of automation isn't the goal; measured lift is. A
second, subtler one: declaring victory off open rates. Without a holdout, what got
measured was sending, not keeping.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: Days 61–90 — Grow
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
**Goal: open the tap, and make every drop land in an audience you own.** Only now does
acquisition spend make sense — and for some products (dev tools especially, Chapter 7)
"the tap" is content, launches, and community rather than paid ads at all.

**What to take from it:**

* Show up with your *own* plan — if you don't, someone who's never held your role will
  hand you theirs. The structure gets more prescriptive early and looser as you learn,
  the mirror image of this chapter's gates easing from day-0 checklists to a day-180
  rhythm.
* The 30–60 window produces two artifacts worth stealing: a first-cut strategy
  (goals → levers → tactical experiments, plus milestones and named threats) and an
  operating manual for how growth actually works — the same "put the ritual on paper"
  move as Day 0 here.
* By day 60–90 the job is to re-plan from evidence: revise the roadmap, refine the model
  and the dashboards — which is exactly what your day-90 gate hands off to the Compound
  phase.

**Exit criteria.** Traffic is flowing from a channel you chose on purpose;
conversions are tracked accurately enough to optimise on (not Pixel-alone guesswork);
and a visible share of new users end up in a channel you own — a verified email, a
linked Discord or Telegram — rather than only in an ad platform's audience you rent.

**The pattern to watch for** is scaling spend before the measurement is trustworthy.
When EMQ is low or events are missing, the ad platform optimises on noise — and the
tuition comes out of your budget. A day spent on conversion quality routinely beats a
week of creative testing.

Before you commit a budget, check the arithmetic holds against your runway — a channel
that works but pays back slower than you can wait is still a cash problem, not just a
growth one:

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: Days 91–180 — Compound
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
The first 90 days build the machine. The next 90 turn it into a *culture* — the
difference between a system that ran once and one that gets a little better every
week. Three threads run in parallel:

**1. An experimentation program, not occasional experiments.** Keep a scored backlog
of growth hypotheses — Sean Ellis's
[ICE](https://www.productplan.com/glossary/ice-scoring-model) (impact, confidence,
ease) is the standard scoring scheme, [PIE](https://conversion.com/framework/pie-framework/)
a close alternative — and run a steady tempo of tests from the top of it. Ellis's
[high-tempo testing](https://www.saastr.com/sean-ellis-founder-ceo-of-growthhackers-building-a-company-wide-growth-culture-video-transcript/)
argument is that learning rate, not any single win, is what compounds; Reforge's
[experiment management system](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-experiment-management-system)
is the same idea with a template and a velocity metric attached. Temper the tempo with
discipline: [not everything deserves an experiment](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/not-running-an-experiment),
and an underpowered test that "wins" is [worse than no test](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/the-ultimate-guide-to-ab-testing).

**2. Prove the program pays.** By day 180 you should be reporting **incremental lift**
from the lifecycle program (global holdout, Chapter 5) and **blended CAC** including
the referral loop's contribution — is growth getting cheaper as the owned audience
grows? That's the flywheel from Chapter 8 showing up in the finances.

**3. Decide what you are before you scale.** Real acquisition money is worth gating on
evidence of product-market fit — the
[Sean Ellis 40% test](https://pmfsurvey.com/) ("how disappointed would you be if this
product disappeared?") is the standard instrument. It's also worth running the system
yourself for a while before hiring a head of growth to run it for you; First Round's
[founder-led growth playbook](https://review.firstround.com/founder-led-growth-playbook/)
makes the case that premature growth hires fail precisely because the founder skipped
the loop you've just spent 90 days building.

The 40% test is worth actually running, not just citing — survey your active users and
put the numbers in:

**Exit criteria — and the real finish line.** There isn't one, and that's the point:
by day 180 the plan should have dissolved into an operating rhythm (Chapter 9's
cadence) that doesn't need this chapter anymore. The observable test: growth work
appears in every sprint, decisions cite the funnel, and the team can name last month's
biggest learning from a *failed* experiment without embarrassment.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: What good looks like at each gate
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
Held deliberately loose — your product's numbers are your own (Chapter 2) — but these
are the questions each gate should answer with a yes:

| Gate | The question | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| Day 30 | Do we trust the data? | Funnel + retention read aloud, replays match events |
| Day 60 | Does the bucket hold? | Holdout-measured lift on activation or early retention |
| Day 90 | Is acquisition landing in owned channels? | Conversion quality good; % of signups verified/linked |
| Day 180 | Is the loop compounding? | Test tempo steady; lift + blended CAC reported monthly |

[YC's key startup metrics](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KR-key-startup-metrics)
is a sane external reference for what investors will eventually ask of the same
numbers; [Reforge on North Star metrics](https://www.reforge.com/blog/north-star-metrics)
for keeping the one metric honest as the machine grows.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
Three things worth doing while the plan is in front of you:

1. **Day 0 itself.** It's an hour with the whole team, and everything else in the plan
   assumes it happened. The checklist above will keep your place — tick things off as
   they land and they'll be here when you come back.
2. **The four gate reviews, in the calendar** — day 30, 60, 90, 180 from today. A
   30-minute review against the exit criteria at each, with the checklists above open.
   Gates that exist in the calendar get held; gates that exist in a chapter don't.
3. **A look at [your workbook](/workbook)** — everything you've written across the
   course (activation sentence, North Star, tracking plan, hypotheses, these
   checklists) in one place, each entry linked back to the chapter that produced it.
   That page *is* your growth plan now.

And if you're happy to share it — where are you starting from?

***

***

## Go deeper

* [Reforge — Succeed in your new growth leadership role](https://www.reforge.com/guides/succeed-in-your-new-growth-leadership-role) — the professional version of this plan: learn the data, early wins, then scale.
* [Delivering Value — the Head of Growth 90-day plan](https://deliveringvalue.co/growth-essays/head-of-growth) — an operator's 30/60/90 with milestones, good for calibrating yours.
* [First Round Review — the founder-led growth playbook](https://review.firstround.com/founder-led-growth-playbook/) — why you run this loop yourself before hiring for it.
* [YC — Analytics for startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8H-analytics-for-startups) — the day-1-to-30 instrumentation phase, from the source most founders trust.
* [YC — Key startup metrics](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KR-key-startup-metrics) — what the day-30/60/90 numbers get measured against eventually.
* [Sean Ellis — the PMF survey](https://pmfsurvey.com/) — the 40% test that gates the day-180 spend decision.
* [Reforge — the growth experiment management system](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-experiment-management-system) — backlog, template, and velocity metrics for the Compound phase.
* [Lenny Rachitsky & Ronny Kohavi — the ultimate guide to A/B testing](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/the-ultimate-guide-to-ab-testing) — the rigor that keeps a high test tempo honest.
* [Product Marketing Alliance — 30-60-90 plan template](https://www.productmarketingalliance.com/30-60-90-day-plan-template-framework/) — if you want the generic scaffold this chapter specialises.
* [John Doerr — Google's OKR playbook](https://www.whatmatters.com/resources/google-okr-playbook) — a heavier operating cadence for when the team outgrows one weekly meeting.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: The zero-to-one version (if you have no users yet)
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
Everything in this chapter so far assumes you have a funnel to measure. If you're
pre-launch, or you launched last week and the dashboard would show single digits, the
plan doesn't change *order* — it changes *shape*. The order still protects you: chasing
acquisition before you can keep anyone is the leaky-bucket mistake (Chapter 2) whether you
have ten users or ten thousand. But at zero-to-one, the Measure phase isn't "build the
daily dashboard" — it's "find evidence a real person wants this at all".

**What each phase becomes before product-market fit:**

* **Measure → talk to users, instrument the one signal.** With no traffic, quantitative
  dashboards are noise. The measurement that matters is qualitative: problem interviews,
  and watching the first handful of people try the product. Instrument exactly one thing —
  the activation event that would mean "they got it" (Chapter 2) — so the moment you *do*
  have users, you can already see whether they reach value.
* **Keep → make the first ten users successful by hand.** This is the phase Paul Graham
  means by [*Do Things That Don't Scale*](https://paulgraham.com/ds.html): onboard people
  personally, fix their problems over a call, do the unscalable work that a lifecycle
  journey will later automate. You're not building the Keep machine yet — you're learning,
  by hand, what it will eventually need to do.
* **Grow → founder-led distribution, not paid.** Launches, communities, writing, direct
  outreach, being useful in the places your users already are. Paid acquisition into a
  product with no proven retention is the most expensive way to learn you don't have
  product-market fit (Chapter 7). Keep the tap off until the bucket holds a little water.

**The trap at this stage** is building the whole machine before anyone is using it — a
schema registry, eight lifecycle journeys, a paid funnel — because building feels like
progress and talking to five users feels like it isn't. It's the "instrumenting forever"
anti-pattern, moved earlier: the work that looks like growth work is quietly standing in
for the uncomfortable work of finding out whether anyone cares.

**What "good enough to start the real plan" looks like** is a pulse: a small group of
users who would be genuinely disappointed if the product vanished (the seed of the Sean
Ellis 40% test you'll run properly at day 180), and a retention curve — even over a dozen
users — that doesn't fall to zero. That's the flattening from Chapter 2, expressed in
miniature. Chapter 11 maps these stages in full; this atom is just the on-ramp.

Once you have that pulse, start at [Day 0](./02-day-0-commit.mdx) and run the plan as
written. The phases are the same; now you have something real to measure.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: Which version of the plan are you on?
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
The plan is one sequence, but you're not running it from a standing start unless you're
truly at zero. Most teams pick it up somewhere in the middle, and where you pick it up
changes how much time each phase deserves and what its exit gate should say. The order is
fixed; the *weighting* is yours.

* **Pre-launch / just launched.** Phase 1 is "instrument before launch", phase 3 is
  "launch channels", and paid barely features. You spend most of your energy in Measure
  and Keep, by hand (see [the zero-to-one version](./09-zero-to-one.mdx)). The day-30 gate
  isn't "trust the dashboard" so much as "we can see whether the first users reach value".
* **Early traction.** You have users and some retention; the machine is worth building
  properly. This is the plan as written — the four phases, roughly the timings given, with
  the gates read against real numbers. Most of this chapter is aimed here.
* **Scaling a working channel.** You already have one channel that works and cash to
  spend. Measure and Keep are a tune-up, not a build; the real weight moves to the Compound
  phase — an experimentation program, proving incremental lift, and the discipline not to
  over-scale before the evidence supports it. Your gates are about *efficiency* (blended
  CAC, payback, lift), not existence.

The one rule that survives every stage: **don't skip a phase because your stage lets you
feel past it.** A scaling team that never verified its measurement is optimising on noise;
a just-launched team that jumps to ads is buying churn. The gates exist precisely to catch
the stage-appropriate version of that mistake.

Chapter 11 goes deep on the stages — the one question that matters at each, the metric
that lies at each, and what "good enough to advance" looks like. If you're unsure which
version of the plan you're on, [read the stages first](../11-stages-of-growth) and come
back; it's the map to this chapter's route.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: Who's running this — and how the plan bends to fit
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
Growth is a hat before it's a hire (Chapter 0), so the person wearing it shapes how the
plan actually runs. The sequence doesn't change — but knowing where *you* will be slow
lets you plan for it instead of stalling there.

* **The solo / non-technical founder.** Your risk is the instrumentation phase: the
  measure step has real engineering in it. Lean on autocapture and no-code installs
  (Chapter 3), keep the tracking plan to ten events, and treat "good enough to trust" as
  the bar, not "complete". Your advantage is the Keep phase — you can do the unscalable
  onboarding by hand, and you already know the customer. Delegate or contract the pipeline
  work; don't let it become the thing that stops you reaching the dashboard.
* **The technical cofounder.** Instrumentation is your comfort zone, which is exactly the
  trap: the "instrumenting forever" anti-pattern (from the Measure phase) is most seductive for engineers,
  because building the perfect event pipeline feels better than reading the imperfect one.
  Time-box Measure hard. Your real growth edge is that Keep and Grow are *engineering* here
  — journeys as code, holdouts, first-party tracking — so the leverage is in shipping the
  loop, not polishing the ingestion.
* **The first growth hire.** You've landed into someone else's product and data. Your first
  30 days are mostly learning — the funnel, the activation metric, what's already been
  tried — before you commit to projects; this chapter's Measure phase *is* your onboarding
  ([Reforge's first-90-days guide](https://www.reforge.com/guides/succeed-in-your-new-growth-leadership-role)
  is the professional version). Show up with your own version of this plan rather than
  waiting to be handed one, and bank a visible early win in activation or retention before
  reaching for acquisition.
* **The marketer learning to code.** Your instinct is acquisition; the plan deliberately
  makes you earn it. Your growth is in getting comfortable reading a retention curve and a
  holdout — the measurement literacy that turns "I ran campaigns" into "I run a loop"
  (Chapter 14). The code you need for the Keep phase is smaller than it looks; the judgement
  is the hard part, and you already have half of it.

Whoever holds it, the plan works best when the *hard part for that person* is named out
loud and supported — a contractor for the pipeline, a time-box on instrumentation, a
mentor for the measurement — rather than left to quietly become the place the plan dies.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: The weekly growth review (your operating ritual)
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
The single most load-bearing commitment in the whole plan is the one that looks smallest:
the recurring 30-minute growth review you booked on Day 0. A plan is a document; a review
is a *ritual*, and the ritual is what Chapter 9 argues separates a growth team from a team
that ran a growth project once and moved on. Meetings that exist in the calendar get held;
intentions that live in a chapter don't.

Keep it to 30 minutes with a fixed agenda, so it survives busy weeks:

Two disciplines keep it honest. **It's a review, not a status theatre** — the point is to
*decide* (keep going, change course, kill something), not to admire dashboards. And **the
losing experiments get airtime**, because a review where only wins are reported quietly
teaches the team to stop taking risks, and the learning rate is the thing that compounds
(Chapter 12). A team that can name last week's most useful *failed* test without
embarrassment is running the loop the way it's meant to run.

The most useful thing a review can have is a standing opening question — one sentence the
meeting always starts with, that points everyone at the same goal. "Is the bucket holding?"
early on; "Is growth getting cheaper?" once the flywheel is turning. Pick yours.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: The experiment doc (a template you can reuse)
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
The [Compound phase](./06-days-91-180-compound.mdx) asks for a steady tempo of experiments, each with a hypothesis,
a primary metric, and a written result — *including the losers*. The way to make that
routine rather than heroic is a template small enough to fill in before you start and
after you finish. Chapter 12 covers the craft of running experiments well; this is the
one-screen artifact you reuse each time.

The fields that force clarity before you spend the traffic:

* **Hypothesis.** *We believe \[change] for \[audience] will cause \[outcome], because
  \[reasoning].* If you can't finish the sentence, the experiment isn't ready.
* **Primary metric + guardrails.** One metric decides it; a couple of guardrails make sure
  you didn't win it by breaking something else. Watching ten metrics means the winner is
  whichever one moved by chance.
* **Minimum detectable effect + how long it runs.** Decided *up front*, so you don't peek
  and stop the moment it looks good — an underpowered "win" is worse than no test
  (Chapter 12).
* **Result + decision.** Written after: what happened, what you learned, and ship / kill /
  iterate. The losers are the point as much as the winners.

Which experiment to run first is a scoring question, not a loudest-voice question. Score
each idea by impact, confidence, and ease and pull from the top:

Then draft the experiment itself in the template — one screen, reused every time:

Keep the finished docs somewhere [the weekly review](./12-the-weekly-review.mdx) can see them. A backlog of
scored ideas and a shelf of written results — wins and losses — *is* the experimentation
program; everything else is just remembering to look at it.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: After 180 days — the next horizon
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
By day 180 the plan should have dissolved into a rhythm that no longer needs this chapter
(the [Compound phase](./06-days-91-180-compound.mdx) is where it starts to). That's the
finish line, and it's the start of the next stretch. Three moves define
the six months after:

* **Add the second channel — only once the first genuinely works.** A single working
  channel is a single point of failure, and platforms change their rules on their own
  schedule. But adding a channel before the first is measured and profitable just splits
  your attention across two things you don't understand yet. The signal to diversify is a
  channel with a stable, known CAC and payback — then you add the next one deliberately,
  and hold it to the same gate. Brian Balfour's
  [growth-loops framing](https://brianbalfour.com/four-fits-growth-framework) is the map for
  which channels actually fit your model rather than just being available.
* **Your first growth hire — now that you know what to hire for.** Having run the loop
  yourself, you can write the job description from experience and evaluate a candidate's
  answers against reality instead of buzzwords. First Round's
  [founder-led growth playbook](https://review.firstround.com/founder-led-growth-playbook/)
  makes the case that premature growth hires fail precisely because the founder skipped the
  loop and can't tell a good hire from a plausible one. Hire to *scale* the loop you
  understand, never to *skip* it.
* **Raise the tempo and defend the flywheel.** The experimentation program (Chapter 12) is
  where compounding now lives — more tests, faster learning. And the owned-audience flywheel
  (Chapter 8) should be showing up in the finances: blended CAC falling as referrals and the
  owned channel grow. The job shifts from *building* the machine to *defending* it — keeping
  the culture and the loop intact as the team grows (Chapter 11's scaling stage is about
  exactly this).

What you care about also changes. The first 180 days were about *existence* — does the
data hold, does the bucket hold, does acquisition land in owned channels. From here it's
about *efficiency and defensibility*: is growth getting cheaper, is the moat (owned
audience, retention, referral) getting deeper, and can the machine survive more people
running it.

One more thing, and it's not about the company. Running this loop end to end is rare and
valuable — it's the difference between having done a growth project and being able to run a
growth program. That's career capital, and [Chapter 14](../14-growth-as-career-capital) is
about what to do with it.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: 10 — Your 30/60/90/180-day plan
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
> **In this chapter:** the course turned into a plan you can actually run — day 0
> commitments, then four phases (measure, keep, grow, compound) each with a goal, a
> checklist, exit criteria, and the anti-patterns that blow it up. The checklists and
> writing exercises below are interactive: everything you tick or write saves to
> [your workbook](/workbook) and is here when you come back.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into fourteen short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read, and every checklist, calculator, and writing box you touch saves to
[your workbook](/workbook). A few pieces carry an operator's video — watch it (or read the
transcript underneath), then read the short digest so the point sticks. Work through them
in order, or jump to what you need:

1. [How to use this plan](./01-how-to-use.mdx) — phases are gates, not dates; protect the order.
2. [Which version are you on?](./10-which-plan-are-you-on.mdx) — the same plan, weighted by your stage.
3. [Who's running this](./11-role-based-plans.mdx) — how it bends to a founder, a cofounder, a first hire, a marketer.
4. [The zero-to-one version](./09-zero-to-one.mdx) — the pre-product-market-fit shape, if you have no users yet.
5. [Day 0 — commit](./02-day-0-commit.mdx) — one hour, no code: name the owner, agree the order.
6. [Days 1–30 — Measure](./03-days-1-30-measure.mdx) — see the truth about your funnel.
7. [Days 31–60 — Keep](./04-days-31-60-keep.mdx) — plug the biggest leaks before you spend on traffic.
8. [Days 61–90 — Grow](./05-days-61-90-grow.mdx) — open the tap into an audience you own.
9. [Days 91–180 — Compound](./06-days-91-180-compound.mdx) — turn the machine into a culture.
10. [The weekly growth review](./12-the-weekly-review.mdx) — the 30-minute ritual that keeps the plan alive.
11. [The experiment doc](./13-the-experiment-doc.mdx) — a reusable template, and how to score what to run next.
12. [What good looks like at each gate](./07-what-good-looks-like.mdx) — the four yes/no gate questions.
13. [After 180 days](./14-after-180-days.mdx) — the second channel, the first hire, defending the flywheel.
14. [Before you move on](./08-before-you-move-on.mdx) — commit to a start date, then the quiz.

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.title: Why stage matters
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.text:
A growth lever isn't good or bad in the abstract. It's good or bad *for your stage*.
Paid acquisition into a product that retains is a machine that turns money into a bigger
active base. The same spend, run three months earlier — before the product keeps
anyone — buys churn at ad-market prices. Nothing about the lever changed; the bucket
underneath it did (Chapter 2).

That's the whole idea of this chapter. At every stage of a product's life, one thing is
the binding constraint — the single problem that, if unsolved, makes work on everything
else wasted. Pre-launch, it's whether anyone wants the thing at all. Just after launch,
it's whether new users reach value. Later, it's whether one channel repeats. "What to
care about" changes because the constraint moves, and pointing your effort at last
stage's constraint is how a well-run team still stalls.

This is also why copying a later-stage company's playbook is a trap. The case study you
read — a Series B company's referral loop, its paid-and-lifecycle engine, its
growth-team org chart — is the answer to a constraint that company had *at the time it
wrote the essay*. Run it against your constraint and you spend the one resource you can't
refill, time, on the wrong problem. Two forces make this worse: the playbooks you can
read are written by the companies that survived (you never see the identical playbook
that sank an earlier-stage startup), and they're written after the fact, once the messy
pre-fit years have been tidied into a clean narrative. Paul Graham's
[*Do Things That Don't Scale*](https://paulgraham.com/ds.html) is the counter-argument
in one essay: the early playbook is *supposed* to look nothing like the scaled one.

So the honest first move isn't picking a tactic — it's naming where you actually are.
The rest of this chapter is one piece per stage. Read your stage first.

**What to take from it:**

* Front-load the "Goldilocks zone" — projects that are both high-impact *and* squarely
  inside your existing skill set — to bank a few quick wins before reaching for unfamiliar
  bets. The same logic as picking the lever that fits your stage, not the one that worked
  for someone else's.
* The plan is deliberately more prescriptive early (a learning roadmap: the product, the
  customers, the growth model) and looser later — you diagnose before you prescribe, the
  personal version of "name where you are before you pick a tactic."
* Guard capacity on purpose. The two things that derail a strong start are picking the
  wrong projects and saying yes to everything until you burn out — so sequence the work,
  don't run it all in parallel.

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.title: Idea and pre-launch: does anyone want this?
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.text:
At this stage you have no users, which means you have almost nothing quantitative worth
measuring. A dashboard built now charts noise: ten signups from friends, a launch-list
that clicked out of politeness. The instinct to instrument early is right for *after*
launch (Chapter 1) — here it's a way to feel productive while dodging the only question
that counts.

**The one question: do people actually want this?** Not "would this be cool" — whether a
real person has the problem badly enough to change what they do about it. The instrument
is conversation: problem interviews, watching how people cope today, and the strongest
signal of all — someone already hacking a workaround, because a workaround is demand that
existed before you showed up. Paul Graham's
[*How to Get Startup Ideas*](https://paulgraham.com/startupideas.html) is the reference
for finding a real problem rather than a plausible-sounding one.

**The trap has two faces.** One is building in the dark: months of code, no
conversations, a launch that meets a market that was never there. The other is premature
vanity metrics — treating landing-page signups or waitlist size as proof of demand, when
an email address costs nothing to give and predicts almost nothing. CB Insights'
running analysis of startup post-mortems repeatedly puts *"no market need"* at or near
the top of why startups die *(directional — the exact share moves cohort to cohort, but
the rank is stable)*. Both faces of the trap are ways of not asking the question.

**What "good enough to advance" looks like:** you can name the problem, name the person
who has it, and you've talked to enough of them that the demand is observed rather than
assumed. You're not looking for a statistically clean survey — you're looking for a
handful of people who'd be genuinely put out if your solution disappeared, and ideally
one or two using a rough version already. That's the ground the next stage stands on.

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.title: Just launched: hunt the activation signal
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.text:
Launch turns the pre-launch question — *does anyone want this?* — into a sharper one you
can finally measure: **do the people who show up reach the point where it's useful?**
That point is activation, the first time a new user feels the product's value (Chapter 2).
It's the one metric that matters now, and it's worth protecting from the two numbers that
will try to stand in for it.

**The metric: activation, not signups.** A signup is a person raising their hand; an
activation is a person getting something out of it. Early on the gap between them is
enormous and it's where nearly every launched product leaks. If you only watch one number
this stage, watch the share of new users who hit your first-value moment — and use
Chapter 2's method to *find* that moment rather than borrowing someone else's. You don't
need a full funnel yet; you need this one step measured honestly.

**The traps, both tempting at launch.** The first is celebrating signups: the launch-day
spike feels like proof, but a spike of people who never come back is a vanity metric with
good timing. The second is over-instrumenting — freezing the launch to build the perfect
event schema, tracking forty actions when you can't yet read the one that matters.
Chapter 1's warning applies double here: thirty events you don't look at are worth less
than one you act on.

**What "good enough to advance" looks like:** a repeatable activation moment — you can say
what it is, you capture it as an event, and a meaningful share of new users reach it
without you hand-holding each one. A few of them come back a second time unprompted. That
early return is the first faint signal of the thing the next stage is entirely about:
whether the product retains.

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.title: Finding product-market fit
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.text:
Product-market fit is the stage everything before it was setting up and everything after
it amplifies. The term was popularised by Marc Andreessen, who described it bluntly as the
only thing that matters before you have it, and the only thing worth working on until you
do. The hard part is that it's a feeling founders describe in hindsight — *"the market
pulling the product out of the startup"* — so the useful move is to make it measurable.
Three readings, from cheapest to most rigorous:

**1. The retention curve that flattens.** Plot the share of a signup cohort still active
by week, and watch the shape (Chapter 2, Chapter 4). If it decays to zero, no amount of
acquisition saves it — you're refilling a bucket with no bottom. If it *flattens above
zero*, some group made the product a habit, and that plateau is fit expressed as data.
This is the reading you already have the instrument for.

**2. The Sean Ellis 40% test.** Survey users with one question — *"how would you feel if
you could no longer use this product?"* — and measure the share who answer *"very
disappointed."* Ellis found, across roughly a hundred startups, that products clearing
about **40%** tended to have the pull to grow, and those under it usually didn't
*(rule of thumb — a useful benchmark, not a law of physics)*. Run yours:

**3. Rahul Vohra's PMF engine.** Superhuman's founder turned that one survey into a
system: segment to the users who'd be very disappointed, learn what they value, and steer
the roadmap to convert the "somewhat disappointed" fence-sitters into them — raising the
40% number deliberately rather than waiting for it. His
[First Round essay](https://review.firstround.com/how-superhuman-built-an-engine-to-find-product-market-fit/)
is the fullest write-up of treating PMF as a process, not a lightning strike.

**The trap here is the most expensive in the course: scaling spend before fit.** Paid
acquisition amplifies whatever exists — into a product with pull it compounds, into one
people can take or leave it just buys churn faster. Fit is the gate on the tap.

**What "good enough to advance" looks like:** a retention curve that plateaus, ideally
corroborated by the 40% test, on a segment you can name. Not perfection everywhere —
durable pull with *someone*. That's what the next stage tries to make repeatable.

**What to take from it:**

* The felt signal: *with* fit, customers pull the product out of you — "beating a path to
  your door"; *without* it you're pushing a rock uphill, and users are too polite to say no
  while quietly not coming back. The retention curve is that feeling turned into data.
* Finding it is iteration, not inspiration. Weebly took 18 months, and Rusenko budgets for
  roughly *10× the iterations you expect* — so listen to customers' **problems**, not their
  proposed solutions, and keep burn low enough to afford that many loops.
* His fit signals line up with this atom's readings: returning (cohort) usage above all,
  then NPS (~40–50), then paying-renewal rates — and he's blunt that signups are a vanity
  number that flatters a product with no retention underneath it.

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.title: Early traction: make one channel repeat
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.text:
With fit in hand, the job changes from *does this work?* to *can I make it happen again on
purpose?* Early traction is the search for repeatability: a way to bring in activated
users that you can run next month and roughly predict the result. The temptation is to run
everywhere at once. The discipline is the opposite.

**The one metric that matters: one channel that repeats.** Not five channels dabbled in —
one that reliably produces users who activate, at a cost you can live with. Brian
Balfour's [*channel/product fit*](https://brianbalfour.com/essays) argument is that
channels aren't interchangeable: a given product has a small number of channels that
actually suit it, and most of the ones that work for other companies won't work for you.
The book-length version is Weinberg and Mares' *Traction* and its Bullseye framework —
test many channels cheaply, then pour effort into the one that responds. The goal is a
single channel proven before you take on the complexity of a second.

**Unit economics start to matter now.** Pre-fit, cost-per-acquisition was noise; here it
becomes a real constraint, because "repeatable" only counts if you can afford to repeat
it (Chapter 7). Before you call a channel scalable, it has to survive its own numbers:

**The trap: channel-hopping.** Trying six channels for two weeks each teaches you almost
nothing — every channel has a learning curve, and abandoning one before you've climbed it
looks like "it didn't work" when the truth is "I didn't finish." Too many simultaneous
bets also means no single channel gets the attention it needs to actually turn. Depth on
one beats breadth across many at this stage.

**What "good enough to advance" looks like:** one channel you can turn on and roughly
predict what comes out — activated users, at a CAC your model and runway can carry. Now
adding a second channel is a considered next step rather than a scramble, which is exactly
what scaling asks for.

**What to take from it:**

* His own cautionary tale is channel-hopping in miniature: he spent ~$5k on generic,
  top-of-funnel content *before* launch and got "crickets" — not because content doesn't
  work, but because he had no framework for which content to make, so the effort scattered.
* The fix is depth-first. Pick the channel, then prioritise with the five stages of
  awareness and **start at the bottom** — the most-aware buyer (comparison pages,
  alternatives, case studies) — and work up. Bottom-of-funnel content converts soonest, so
  the channel pays back before you've climbed the whole funnel.
* A bonus of going deep on one channel: bottom-of-funnel content doubles as sales
  collateral — it earns its keep even with zero marketing, which is what "a channel that
  repeats" looks like once you've actually committed to it.

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.title: Scaling: build the machine
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.text:
Scaling is where growth stops being a series of wins and becomes a system that produces
them. You have one channel that repeats and a product that keeps people; the work now is
compounding — adding capacity without adding chaos. Three things tend to arrive together.

**A second channel.** With one channel proven and its economics understood, a second is a
calculated diversification rather than a scramble (Chapter 5's warning about too many bets
was for *before* the first one turned). Two working channels also reduce the risk that a
single platform change — an algorithm tweak, a price rise — takes your whole top-of-funnel
with it.

**The first dedicated growth hire.** Until now growth has been a hat someone wears
part-time (Chapter 0). At scale the loop is big enough to be someone's whole job — owning
the funnel, the experiment backlog, and the lifecycle system end to end. Hire *into* a
working loop, not to go find one: a growth hire inherits a machine to run faster, not a
blank page to invent product-market fit on.

**An operating cadence.** The thing that separates a team that scales from a team that had
a good quarter is ritual (Chapter 9): a standing review, a scored experiment backlog, and
decisions that cite the funnel instead of the loudest voice. The cadence is what keeps a
growing team pointed at the same numbers.

**The trap: scaling chaos.** Volume is a magnifier — it makes a good loop bigger and a
broken one break louder. Two specific failures show up here: pouring spend on top of a
retention leak that fit *mostly* fixed but didn't (buying churn again, just at a bigger
scale), and losing the culture — the shared language, the retention-first instinct, the
close contact with users — under the weight of new people and new channels. The machine is
only as good as what it's built on.

**What comes after:** there's no "advance" past scaling — the job becomes keeping the
machine honest as it grows, which is the operating rhythm Chapters 9 and 10 are about.

**What to take from it:**

* The plan assumes you're hired *into* a working growth function, not to invent
  product-market fit. Month one is a learning roadmap — the growth model qualitatively and
  quantitatively, jobs-to-be-done, actually using the product — because you inherit a
  machine and learn it before you touch it.
* The days-30–60 output is the operating cadence this atom asks for: a "growth operations
  manual" defining team structure now and 12–18 months out, operating principles for
  decisions made when you're not in the room, and how the growth team's rituals differ from
  the rest of the company.
* By days 60–90 the job is to re-plan from evidence — revise the strategy, refine the model
  and the dashboards as reality corrects your first guesses. That's the "keep the machine
  honest as it grows" work scaling never finishes.

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.title: Metrics that lie by stage
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.text:
A metric doesn't lie on its own — it lies *at a stage*, when it's the wrong reading for the
constraint you're actually facing. The same signup count that's meaningless at launch is a
perfectly good top-of-funnel input later, once activation and retention underneath it are
solid. The skill is knowing which number is quietly standing in for the one that matters.

The pattern runs the length of the arc. **Pre-launch**, waitlist and landing-page signups
pose as demand — but an email address is free to give and predicts almost nothing.
**At launch**, the signup count and the launch-day spike pose as traction, when a spike of
people who never return is activity with good timing. **Finding fit**, revenue can rise
while the base churns underneath it — new sales mask a leaky bucket, so read retention
alongside MRR, never instead of it. **Early traction**, blended CAC averages cheap organic
in with expensive paid, making paid look affordable when it isn't — break CAC out by
channel before you scale one. **At scale**, opens and engagement pose as proof a journey
works, but activity isn't lift; only a holdout attributes any change to the journey
(Chapter 5).

Underneath all of them is Eric Ries' distinction from *The Lean Startup* between *vanity*
and *actionable* metrics. A vanity number tends to only go up (cumulative totals never
fall), can't be tied to a decision, and measures activity instead of an outcome.
Actionable numbers are rates, they're comparable over time, and they change what you'd
actually do next. When a chart makes you feel good but wouldn't change a decision, treat
that as a warning.

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.text:
The arc of this chapter is one idea repeated: at every stage a different thing is the
binding constraint, so what to care about changes as you grow — and the fastest way to
stall is to run a later stage's playbook against an earlier stage's problem. Each stage
comes down to one honest question:

* **Idea and pre-launch** — *Does anyone actually want this, enough to change what they
  do?* The instrument is conversation, not a dashboard.
* **Just launched** — *Do the people who show up reach value, or do they just sign up?*
  Activation, not the signup count.
* **Finding product-market fit** — *Would my users be genuinely disappointed if this
  vanished — and does the retention curve flatten?* Pull, read as data.
* **Early traction** — *Can I turn on one channel and roughly predict what comes out?*
  Repeatability, held to its unit economics.
* **Scaling** — *Is the machine compounding on something solid, or is volume hiding a
  leak?* Capacity without chaos.

And running underneath all five: *which stage am I actually in — and am I solving its
constraint or borrowing someone else's answer?*

***

## Go deeper

* [YC — Startup Library](https://www.ycombinator.com/library) — the founder-trusted playbooks, sortable by the stage you're in.
* [Paul Graham — Do Things That Don't Scale](https://paulgraham.com/ds.html) — why the early-stage playbook is *supposed* to look nothing like the scaled one.
* [Paul Graham — Startup = Growth](https://paulgraham.com/growth.html) — what "growth" actually means, and why stage frames every decision about it.
* [Paul Graham — How to Get Startup Ideas](https://paulgraham.com/startupideas.html) — finding a real problem worth the pre-launch stage.
* [Sean Ellis — the PMF survey](https://pmfsurvey.com/) — the 40% test that gates the tap, from the source.
* [Rahul Vohra — how Superhuman built an engine to find product-market fit](https://review.firstround.com/how-superhuman-built-an-engine-to-find-product-market-fit/) — treating PMF as a repeatable process, not a lightning strike.
* [Brian Balfour — essays](https://brianbalfour.com/essays) — channel/product fit and why the channel that worked for another company probably won't work for you.
* [Lenny's Newsletter](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/) — operator essays across every stage, including how to find your activation metric.
* Marc Andreessen — "The Only Thing That Matters" (2007) — the essay that popularised the term product-market fit (widely archived; search the title).
* Gabriel Weinberg & Justin Mares — *Traction* — the Bullseye framework for testing many channels cheaply and committing to the one that responds.

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth.title: Chapter 11 — Stages of growth: what to care about when
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth.text:
> **In this chapter:** why a growth lever that helps at one stage hurts at another,
> the five stages a product moves through — idea, just-launched, finding
