> product-market fit, early traction, and scaling — and for each one the single
> question worth answering, the one metric that matters, the trap most teams fall
> into, and what "good enough to advance" looks like. Then a synthesis of the
> metrics that mislead at every stage. By the end you'll be able to name where you
> actually are, and stop borrowing a later-stage company's answers to questions you
> don't have yet.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into eight short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. The middle five walk the stages in order; the shape repeats each
time (the question, the metric, the trap, the advance criteria) so the pattern sticks.
Work through them in order, or jump to the stage you're in:

1. [Why stage matters](./01-why-stage-matters.mdx) — the same lever helps here and hurts there.
2. [Idea and pre-launch](./02-idea-and-pre-launch.mdx) — one question, almost no dashboards.
3. [Just launched](./03-just-launched.mdx) — hunt the activation signal, not the signup count.
4. [Finding product-market fit](./04-finding-pmf.mdx) — the flattening curve and the 40% test.
5. [Early traction](./05-early-traction.mdx) — one channel that works before you add a second.
6. [Scaling](./06-scaling.mdx) — the machine, the first hire, and keeping the loop.
7. [Metrics that lie by stage](./07-metrics-that-lie-by-stage.mdx) — which number to distrust when.
8. [Before you move on](./08-before-you-move-on.mdx) — the honest question per stage, then a quiz.

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: What deserves an experiment
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
An experiment is not free. It costs **traffic** — you split it, so each variant learns
slower and you need more users to reach an answer. It costs **time** — design, build,
wait for significance, read. And it costs **opportunity** — the change you didn't ship
while this one ran. Testing is a way to *buy certainty* about a decision, and like
anything you buy, it's only worth it when the certainty is worth more than the price.

Two questions decide it:

* **Is the change cheap and reversible?** Then ship it. If a clearer button label is
  obviously better and trivial to roll back, a two-week test to confirm it is time you
  won't get back — and at startup traffic the effect is probably too small to detect
  anyway (§12.4).
* **Is the decision expensive to get wrong, and hard to undo?** Then test it. Pricing
  changes, removing an onboarding step, a new activation flow — these are the decisions
  where being wrong costs months, and where certainty is worth paying for.

Lenny's [guide to when *not* to run an experiment](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/not-running-an-experiment)
is a good checklist: ship without a test when the change is obviously good, too small to
measure at your traffic, or easily reversed; test when the stakes and the uncertainty are
both high.

There's a humbling reason to test the big ones: you're bad at predicting winners, and so
is everyone else. At Microsoft, only about **one-third of experiments moved the metric
they were designed to move** in the intended direction — another third did nothing, and a
third made things worse (Ronny Kohavi's data from Bing/Microsoft) *(well-attested)*. The
famous example is a Bing ad-headline change one engineer left in the backlog for months
because it looked trivial; it turned out to be worth roughly **$100M a year** (Kohavi,
["The Surprising Power of Online Experiments," HBR](https://hbr.org/2017/09/the-surprising-power-of-online-experiments))
*(well-attested)*. If experts guessed right, you wouldn't need the test. The point of
experimentation isn't to rubber-stamp good ideas — it's to catch the two-thirds that
would have quietly cost you.

So the rule isn't "test everything." It's: ship the cheap, reversible, obvious changes
fast, and spend your limited testing capacity on the decisions that are expensive to get
wrong and where your intuition genuinely might be.

**What to take from it:**

* The failure rates *rise* with optimisation: ~two-thirds of ideas fail at Microsoft,
  \~85% at Bing, 92% at Airbnb search — and every team assumes it's the exception until the
  data humbles it. That's the case for testing the expensive decisions: intuition is wrong
  more often than anyone guesses.
* When *not* to test comes down to sample size. Below ~tens of thousands of users the
  statistics only see large effects, and the "magic" starts around 200k — so a small
  startup should test big swings, not button colours (§12.4). Some calls (a one-time,
  irreversible decision) can't be A/B tested at all.
* Two guardrails from the same humility: don't ship on flat (a no-difference result still
  adds code and maintenance cost for no gain — a no-ship, not a free launch), and invoke
  Twyman's law — a result that looks too good is probably a bug, so investigate before you
  celebrate.

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: The hypothesis format
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
An idea like "let's redesign onboarding" isn't testable — there's no prediction in it. A
**hypothesis** is the same idea written so reality can prove it wrong. The format that
forces this:

> **We believe** \[change] **for** \[audience] **will cause** \[outcome], **measured by**
> \[metric], **because** \[reasoning].

Each slot does work:

* **change** — the specific thing you'll build. If you can't name it in one line, it
  isn't ready to test.
* **audience** — who sees it: new signups? mobile users? one segment? A change can win
  for one group and lose for another, so naming the audience keeps you honest about which
  you're measuring.
* **outcome** — the behaviour you expect to shift, *with a direction*. "Increases" or
  "decreases," not "affects."
* **metric** — the single number that will settle it (§12.3). Naming it before you build
  is what stops you picking, afterwards, whichever number happens to look good.
* **because** — your reasoning. This is the most valuable slot: it's the *belief* being
  tested, so when the result comes back you learn whether the belief was right — not just
  whether the button moved.

Writing the "because" first is what separates a program from a pile of A/B tests. A test
pinned to a belief still teaches you something when it fails; a test with no reasoning
leaves you holding a number and no idea what it meant. Teams that skip the reasoning tend
to run the same failed experiment twice, because nobody wrote down *why* they thought it
would work.

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: One primary metric, and guardrails
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
Decide what "success" means before the test runs, and let exactly *one* metric decide it.
Kohavi calls this the **Overall Evaluation Criterion (OEC)** — the single measure the
experiment is optimising, agreed up front. One metric, chosen in advance, is what stops
the after-the-fact hunt for whichever number happens to look good.

The trap is watching everything. If you track fifteen metrics and declare victory when
*any* of them is up, you'll "win" almost every test — some metric is always up by chance
(§12.6). More metrics feels more rigorous; it's the opposite.

But you can't watch *only* the primary metric, because a change that lifts your target can
quietly wreck something else. That's what **guardrail metrics** are for: a short list of
things the test is not allowed to break, whatever it does to the primary. Common
guardrails are revenue, unsubscribe/opt-out rate, latency and error rates, support
tickets, and core retention. A new onboarding flow that lifts activation but doubles the
unsubscribe rate hasn't won — the guardrail caught a bad trade before you shipped it.

Two more properties of a good primary metric:

* **Sensitive enough to move.** Revenue and 30-day retention are true but slow — they
  barely budge inside a two-week test (§12.4). Pick a leading indicator you can actually
  detect moving: activation rate, a key action in week one, the step-completion you're
  targeting.
* **Tied to the hypothesis.** The primary metric is the "measured by" slot from §12.2. If
  the change is about onboarding, the primary metric is an onboarding metric — not a
  downstream number the change only touches faintly, where any movement is mostly noise.

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: Power and sample size
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
**Statistical power** is the chance a test *detects* a real effect when one exists. A test
at low power is a smoke detector with a flat battery: the thing can be happening and the
alarm stays silent. Underpowered tests are the quiet failure mode of small-team
experimentation, and they fail in two ways — they miss real wins (reporting "no
difference" when there was one), and they hand you **false wins** that look significant
but won't replicate. Acting on a false win is worse than running no test at all, because
you ship the change *and* trust it.

Three levers set how much traffic you need:

* **Baseline rate** — where the metric starts.
* **Minimum detectable effect (MDE)** — the smallest change you care to catch. This one
  dominates: required sample grows with roughly the *square* of how small an effect you
  want to see, so detecting a 1% lift needs about **four times** the traffic of detecting
  a 2% lift *(well-attested — it's arithmetic, not opinion)*. Small effects are enormously
  expensive to prove.
* **Confidence and power** — conventionally 95% confidence and 80% power.

The practical consequence for a startup: you usually can't detect small effects, so **test
big swings** — whole onboarding flows, whole journeys, pricing — not button colours. And
decide the sample size and run length *before* you start, with a calculator like
[Evan Miller's](https://www.evanmiller.org/ab-testing/sample-size.html), so "how long do
we run this?" has an answer that isn't "until it looks significant."

Which is the cardinal sin: **peeking.** If you watch a running test and stop the moment it
crosses significance, you badly inflate your false-positive rate — a test with *no* real
effect will touch significance surprisingly often if you check it every day. The p-value
is only trustworthy at the sample size you committed to. Pick the finish line first, then
look when you reach it. Evan Miller's
["How Not to Run an A/B Test"](https://www.evanmiller.org/how-not-to-run-an-ab-test.html)
shows exactly how much peeking distorts the result.

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: Holdouts and incrementality
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
An A/B test compares variant A against variant B of a *single* change. A **holdout** asks
a broader question: is this whole *program* producing anything at all? You keep a random
slice of users out of the thing entirely — no lifecycle emails, no journeys, nothing — and
compare them to everyone who got the full treatment. The gap between the two is
**incremental lift**: the outcome that happened *because* of the program and wouldn't have
happened anyway.

This is the honest answer to the measurement trap from Chapter 5: **opens are activity;
lift is proof.** A 62% open rate tells you the email was sent and rendered. It tells you
nothing about whether the journey *changed* anyone's behaviour — some of those users would
have come back regardless. Only a held-out group, living without the program, shows you
the counterfactual. Without it you can't separate "the journey worked" from "these were
people who'd have converted anyway."

Two flavours worth naming:

* **A/B test** — for a single change. It runs for a while, you ship the winner, and the
  test ends.
* **Global holdout** — a small, *persistent* control (often 1–5% of users) held out of the
  entire lifecycle program continuously, so at any time you can read the program's total
  incremental contribution. This is the day-180 artifact from the plan (Chapter 10):
  incremental lift reported monthly, not open rates.

The cost is real and worth saying out loud: a holdout means deliberately *not* treating
some users, which feels like leaving money on the table. It isn't — it's the price of
knowing whether the program pays for itself. A team that can't say what its lifecycle work
adds *over doing nothing* is flying blind, however good the open rates look.
Incrementality is what turns "we send a lot of email" into "the program is worth $X a
month," and only the second sentence survives a budget review.

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: Reading results honestly
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
Getting a number is easy; not fooling yourself with it is the skill. A few traps, and the
discipline that beats each.

**The multiple-comparisons trap.** Test enough metrics or enough variants and something
will look significant by pure chance — at 95% confidence, roughly one in twenty
comparisons is a false positive even when nothing is going on. Slice a result twenty ways
("it didn't work overall, but look, it won for left-handed users in Canada") and you'll
always find a winner. XKCD [drew this perfectly](https://xkcd.com/882/) with green jelly
beans. The defence is the primary metric you committed to in §12.3: it decides the test;
everything else is a hypothesis for *next* time, not a victory to claim now.

**P-hacking by another name.** Peeking (§12.4), dropping "bad" days, redefining the metric
after seeing the data, running until significant — all of it manufactures wins. The rule:
fix the metric, the audience, the run length, and the success bar *before* the test, then
read what you agreed to read. Kohavi's **Twyman's law** is the right reflex — any figure
that looks surprisingly good is probably wrong until you've ruled out a bug or a fluke.

**Read the losers.** A flat or negative result is not a wasted test — it's most of what
you'll get. Remember only about a third of ideas win *(well-attested; §12.1)*. "No
detectable difference" tells you the effect is smaller than you hoped, which is a real
finding that saves you from shipping the change and believing it did something. The losers
also feed the backlog: *why* didn't it move — wrong audience, too small, or a bad belief?

**Ship, kill, or iterate — and write it down.** Every test ends in one of three decisions:
**ship** it (clear win, guardrails intact), **kill** it (flat or negative), or **iterate**
(promising but inconclusive — sharpen the change and rerun). Then record it: hypothesis,
result, decision, what you'd try next. The write-up costs thirty seconds; re-running a
forgotten experiment costs a month — and a documented failure is the thing a growth team
can say out loud without embarrassment (Chapter 10's finish line).

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: Tempo and the backlog
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
The difference between a team that A/B tests and a team with a *program* is two rituals: a
scored backlog and a steady tempo.

**The backlog.** Every growth idea — a new journey, a pricing change, an onboarding step
removed — goes into one list as a *hypothesis* (§12.2), not a task. Score each on
**[ICE](https://www.productplan.com/glossary/ice-scoring-model)** — impact, confidence,
ease, each 1–10 — and let the scores order the list.
[PIE](https://conversion.com/framework/pie-framework/) (potential, importance, ease) is
the same idea with different letters; either works, and the only real mistake is arguing
frameworks instead of running tests. The score's job isn't precision — it's to make "this
would be huge" meet "how confident are we, actually?", and to give the weekly meeting a
top of the list to pull from instead of the loudest idea in the room.

**The tempo.** Sean Ellis — who coined both "growth hacking" and the North Star framing
this course uses — argues the compounding asset is your **velocity of tests**: more shots
on goal means faster learning, and the learning is what compounds even when individual
tests fail ([high-tempo testing](https://www.saastr.com/sean-ellis-founder-ceo-of-growthhackers-building-a-company-wide-growth-culture-video-transcript/)).
Reforge's [experiment management system](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-experiment-management-system)
puts a number on it — a standard template plus a visible backlog roughly tripled one
team's testing velocity *(directional)*. For a startup, "high tempo" might mean one
experiment a week. That's fine: one a week, every week, with results written down, beats a
burst of ten followed by a quarter of nothing.

The thing that holds it together is a 30-minute **weekly review**: groom the backlog,
launch the top-scored test, read last week's, record the decision.

**What to take from it:**

* Ellis leads with the Amazon framing — Bezos: success is "a function of how many
  experiments we run per day, per month, per year." Volume of tests, not the brilliance of
  any one, is the thing that compounds.
* The mistake he warns against is obsessing over one "perfect" test. Because nobody
  reliably knows which idea wins, three-to-five smaller shots a week beat betting the
  quarter on a single swing — the shots-on-goal (Gretzky) logic behind this atom's tempo.
* Velocity only pays off when it's aimed. He starts every cycle with qualitative analysis
  of *who loves the product and why*, so the backlog is fed by real understanding of value,
  not random ideas — which is the ICE-scored hypothesis list this atom builds.

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
Two ideas hold this chapter together. First, experiments are a *cost* — traffic, time,
opportunity — so you ship the cheap and reversible changes and save testing for decisions
where being wrong is expensive and your intuition genuinely might be (§12.1). Second, a
test is only worth running if you can trust the answer, which means: one hypothesis
(§12.2), one primary metric with guardrails (§12.3), enough power to see the effect
(§12.4), a control that proves incrementality (§12.5), and an honest read that resists the
traps (§12.6). Wrap those in a weekly tempo (§12.7), and the learning rate — not any
single win — is what compounds.

***

***

## Go deeper

* [Ronny Kohavi on Lenny's Podcast — the ultimate guide to A/B testing](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/the-ultimate-guide-to-ab-testing) — trust, invalid results, and when not to test at all, from the person who ran experimentation at Microsoft, Amazon, and Airbnb. ([Spotify](https://open.spotify.com/episode/3vD3c87FT7RKnvZUJRwOb4), [YouTube](https://youtu.be/hEzpiDuYFoE))
* *Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments* (Ronny Kohavi, Diane Tang & Ya Xu) — the standard reference on running A/B tests you can believe: the OEC, guardrails, the peeking problem, Twyman's law, and sample-ratio mismatch.
* [Kohavi — "The Surprising Power of Online Experiments," HBR](https://hbr.org/2017/09/the-surprising-power-of-online-experiments) — the Bing example and the hard evidence that experts can't predict winners.
* [Reforge — the growth experiment management system](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-experiment-management-system) — backlog, template, and velocity metrics that turn occasional tests into a program.
* [Lenny's Newsletter — when *not* to run an experiment](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/not-running-an-experiment) — the checklist for shipping without a test.
* [Sean Ellis on high-tempo testing](https://www.saastr.com/sean-ellis-founder-ceo-of-growthhackers-building-a-company-wide-growth-culture-video-transcript/) — why the velocity of tests is the compounding asset.
* [ProductPlan — the ICE scoring model](https://www.productplan.com/glossary/ice-scoring-model) and [Conversion.com — PIE](https://conversion.com/framework/pie-framework/) — two ways to score the backlog; pick one and stop debating.
* [Evan Miller — "How Not to Run an A/B Test"](https://www.evanmiller.org/how-not-to-run-an-ab-test.html) and his [sample-size calculator](https://www.evanmiller.org/ab-testing/sample-size.html) — the peeking problem, and deciding run length up front.

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: Chapter 12 — Running experiments well
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
> **In this chapter:** which changes earn the fixed cost of a test and which you should
> just ship, the hypothesis format that turns an opinion into something falsifiable, the
> one primary metric and the guardrails that protect the rest of the business, why an
> underpowered "win" is worse than no test at all, holdouts and the incrementality they
> prove, how to read results without fooling yourself, and the ICE-scored backlog and
> weekly tempo that make the learning rate — not any single win — the thing that
> compounds.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into eight short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. The first six build the discipline of a single trustworthy
experiment; the last two turn it into a weekly habit and check you on the whole thing.
Work through them in order, or jump to what you need:

1. [What deserves an experiment](./01-what-deserves-an-experiment.mdx) — every test has a fixed cost; not every change earns it.
2. [The hypothesis format](./02-the-hypothesis-format.mdx) — the sentence that forces clarity before you build.
3. [Primary metric and guardrails](./03-primary-metric-and-guardrails.mdx) — one number to move, a few you refuse to break.
4. [Power and sample size](./04-power-and-sample-size.mdx) — why an underpowered win is worse than no test.
5. [Holdouts and incrementality](./05-holdouts-and-incrementality.mdx) — opens are activity; lift is proof.
6. [Reading results honestly](./06-reading-results-honestly.mdx) — p-hacking, the losers, and ship/kill/iterate.
7. [Tempo and the backlog](./07-tempo-and-the-backlog.mdx) — an ICE-scored ritual, because learning rate compounds.
8. [Before you move on](./08-before-you-move-on.mdx) — recap, flashcards, and a quiz to check yourself.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: Who cares about what
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
The first skill in talking about growth is realising you're not talking to one audience.
Four groups will ask "how's growth going," and each means something different by the
question. Answer them all the same way and you'll bore three of them and mislead the
fourth.

* **Investors and the board.** They operate at the highest altitude: a handful of
  *outcome* metrics that predict whether the company is worth more than last quarter —
  growth rate, retention (and net revenue retention), CAC payback and LTV:CAC, burn and
  runway. Andreessen Horowitz's [16 startup metrics](https://a16z.com/16-startup-metrics/)
  is the canonical list of what this audience reads, and the thing it repeats is *know
  which number you mean* — GMV is not revenue, cumulative is not active. A board update
  is a trend and a few ratios with one narrative, not a tour of your dashboard.
* **Cofounders.** Your altitude exactly: they want the whole picture — the leak you're
  working, the bet you're making, the thing that isn't working yet. This is the one
  audience you can be completely unpolished with, and the one where hiding a problem does
  the most damage, because they'll act on the rosy version too.
* **The team.** They care about the numbers they can actually move *this week* — the
  input metrics: activation rate, journey lift, experiment results, the funnel step
  someone owns. An outcome like MRR is motivating once a quarter and useless as a weekly
  target, because no one can point to the line of work that moves it directly.
* **Customers.** They do not care about your growth numbers at all. They care about the
  outcome *for them*. "10,000 teams use us" reaches a customer only as a trust signal —
  shorthand for "this is safe to rely on." The same number is doing a completely
  different job than it does on a board slide.

The mistake that quietly costs you credibility is **writing one report for all four.**
Send the board your weekly input metrics and they can't find the signal; give the team
the board's tidy outcome summary and they've nothing to act on; show a customer either
and you've bored them. It's the "dashboard graveyard" from Chapter 0 in a new form — the
right data, pointed at the wrong audience, changes no one's mind.

The fix is a habit: before you pick a single number, decide the audience's *altitude* and
the *one thing they're trying to learn*. Everything else in this chapter is how to do
that well. Start by naming it.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: The two-number story
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
A single number is easy to argue with, because on its own it can't show cause.
"Activation is 44%" invites *so what?* — up or down from what, and did anything you did
move it? The unit that actually persuades is a **pair**: a *leading* metric you control,
and the *lagging* outcome it's supposed to move.

* A **leading** metric is an input — something a person on the team can act on this week.
  Onboarding completion, welcome-journey lift, invites sent per new user. It moves
  quickly, and it's yours to change.
* A **lagging** metric is an outcome — retention, revenue, the North Star. It moves
  slowly, it's what the business is actually made of, and it isn't directly movable: you
  reach it *through* the inputs. (This is the North Star property from Chapter 2 — it
  reflects value and you work on 3–5 input metrics beneath it — pointed at how you *talk*
  about it.)

The story lives in the gap between them. "We shipped the onboarding checklist;
completion went from 51% to 68% (leading, controllable), and week-4 retention on that
cohort is +3 points (lagging, the outcome we care about)." (Numbers illustrative.) That
sentence persuades because it names the thing you did, the input it moved, and the
outcome that followed — a mechanism, not a coincidence. One number can't carry a
mechanism; two, with the causal link stated between them, can.

It also protects you both ways. A leading metric that moved with no lagging follow-through
yet is an honest "not proven yet" — you did the work, the outcome hasn't landed, keep
watching. A lagging metric that moved with no leading metric behind it is a *warning*: you
can't explain why, so you can't repeat it, and you shouldn't take credit. a16z's rule for
the board version — [know exactly which metric you mean](https://a16z.com/16-startup-metrics/)
— is the same discipline underneath: the pair is only honest if each half is defined
precisely enough to check.

The anti-pattern to retire is presenting the outcome alone as proof of your work.
"Revenue is up" is true and unattributable. "We did X, the input moved, revenue followed"
is a claim you can stand behind — or be proven wrong on, which is exactly the point of
making it.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: Two cadences, two reports
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
The audiences from the last piece map to two standing reports, on two rhythms. Keeping
them separate is what stops the weekly meeting from ballooning and the board update from
drowning in detail.

**The weekly team review (operational).** Thirty minutes, a fixed agenda, input metrics
and live work — the growth ritual from Chapter 9. Its job is *decisions and learnings
this week*, not status. Brian Balfour's [weekly growth meeting](https://brianbalfour.com/essays/growth-meeting),
run at HubSpot and Reforge, is the template worth compressing: anomalies get an owner
rather than a live group debugging session, because group debugging is how a 30-minute
meeting becomes a two-hour one.

**The monthly or board update (outcomes).** An hour to write, once a month (quarterly for
the board) — the zoom-out from Chapter 9. It carries outcomes and trend, not this week's
inputs: the North Star trend, retention, incremental lift from the lifecycle program (the
global holdout), blended CAC and the referral loop's contribution, and exactly one
learning worth the reader's attention. The rule of thumb: a board update is a **trend and
a handful of ratios with one narrative**, never a data dump. (Rule of thumb.)

The two reports share almost no rows, on purpose. If your board update reads like your
weekly review, you've handed the highest-altitude audience the lowest-altitude data — and
asked them to find the signal you were supposed to find for them. The next pieces are how
to make the few numbers that survive to the board update honest ones.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: How do you know it worked?
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
Sooner or later someone — an investor, a skeptical cofounder, a numbers-literate
customer — asks the one question that separates a growth team from a marketing team: *how
do you know it worked?* How do you know the welcome journey *caused* the retention bump,
rather than those users being the kind who'd have stuck around anyway?

You already have the honest answer from Chapter 5: a **holdout.** The skill here is saying
it without a statistics lecture, because the moment you reach for "p-values" and
"confidence intervals" you've lost the room. The version that lands is plain and concrete:

> "Of everyone who qualified for the welcome journey, we held back a random slice — say
> 10% — and sent them nothing. Everyone else got the emails. A month later the messaged
> group had upgraded at 18%, the held-back group at 11%. That 7-point gap is what the
> journey added, because the two groups were identical except for the emails." (Numbers
> illustrative.)

**What to take from it:**

* Same idea as this atom's holdout, in a paid-media frame: a *geo holdout* — turn media off
  in a few matched regions, leave it on everywhere else, and the revenue gap is the
  channel's true contribution. It sits at the top of the measurement pyramid precisely
  because it's a controlled experiment, not attribution laid over history.
* Why lift beats activity: platform-reported ROAS and *incremental* ROAS routinely diverge.
  In their example a three-state holdout showed Meta was actually *under*-reporting, while
  Google brand search's incremental value was only ~23% of the reported number — the
  platform taking credit for buyers who'd have converted anyway. Same trap as crediting a
  journey with conversions it didn't cause.
* A detail that defuses the skeptic's fear: the "held-back" spend isn't burned — it's
  reallocated to the live regions, so the test costs almost nothing while buying a causal
  read. The control group is a measurement tool, not lost money.

Three moves make that explanation work:

* **Lead with the control group, not the math.** The intuition people already have is the
  drug-trial one: some patients get the placebo. "We kept a group who got nothing, on
  purpose" is a sentence anyone follows. Reforge and Eppo call this *incrementality*; your
  stakeholder doesn't need the word, they need the placebo.
* **Name the gap as the credit.** Not "the journey has an 18% conversion rate" (that's the
  messaged group alone, and it includes people who'd have converted anyway) but "the
  journey *added* 7 points over doing nothing." The incremental number is smaller and far
  more defensible — [Eppo's incrementality primer](https://www.geteppo.com/blog/marketing-incrementality-101)
  is the fuller version for anyone who wants it.
* **Say what would have changed your mind.** "If the held-back group had converted at the
  same rate, we'd have killed the journey." A stakeholder trusts a measurement far more
  once they hear it *could* have come out against you — that's the tell that you weren't
  just building a case.

The reason this conversation is worth rehearsing: it's the whole distance between "we sent
3,400 emails" (activity) and "the program added 7 points of activation, measured against a
control" (proof). The first invites more questions; the second ends the debate — which is
exactly what you want a report to do.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: Presenting a loss
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
Most experiments lose. Ronny Kohavi, who ran experimentation at Microsoft, reports that
only about **a third** of well-designed experiments there improved the metric they were
built to move; many of the rest did nothing, and some made it worse. (Well-attested, from
Kohavi's work at Bing and Microsoft.) At other top experimentation teams the win rate is
often quoted lower still. (Directional.) If most tests fail *at the companies best at
testing*, a losing result on your team is not a failure of the team — it's the normal
output of a working experiment loop.

That reframe is the whole conversation: a loss is a **learning you already paid for,** and
the payment is non-refundable. The only way to waste it is to hide it. So the way you
present a loss matters more than the loss:

* **State the hypothesis first, then the result.** "We believed a second onboarding email
  would lift activation; it didn't move — flat within noise." The hypothesis makes the
  loss legible: you tested a belief, and now you know something. A result with no stated
  hypothesis just looks like something broke.
* **Say what it ruled out.** A flat result narrows the search: "so the activation leak
  isn't email volume — it's earlier, in the first-run experience." That sentence is the
  asset the experiment bought. Chapter 9's rule — *write the losers down too* — exists
  because a learning that lives in one head gets re-paid-for six months later.
* **Show your tempo, not just this test.** One loss in isolation looks like a miss; the
  same loss as "test 7 of the quarter, here's what the last six taught us" looks like a
  machine working. Sean Ellis's case for high-tempo testing is exactly this: velocity
  compounds *because* individual tests fail, not despite it.

**What to take from it:**

* Capland makes failure safe *structurally*, not with vibes: write the team's values into
  an operating manual and make the first one an explicit "we experiment to find out what
  works and what doesn't" — so a loss is the process working, not a person failing.
* Make it real by launching it like a feature (to execs, then the team, then the company)
  and reinforcing it weekly — planning meetings and 1:1s that ask "what did you get to
  experiment on this week?" Reporting a loss becomes a routine answer, not an admission.
* The accelerant is the leader dogfooding failure: standing up in front of the company with
  an experiment where their *own* hypothesis was dead wrong and what it taught them. That's
  what gives everyone else permission to present a flat result — the exact move this atom
  turns on.

What you must never do: bury the loss, quietly stop reporting a journey that isn't
working, or go hunting through the data for the one sub-segment where it "won" so you have
a positive to show. That last one — p-hacking a win out of a loss — is the fastest way to
lose a numbers-literate stakeholder for good, because they *can* smell it, and once
they've smelled it once, every future win you present gets discounted too.

The counter-intuitive payoff: a leader who stands up and presents their own flat result —
what Andrew Capland calls dogfooding failure — buys more credibility than a clean win
would. It's proof the numbers aren't being managed for the audience, and that proof is the
only thing that makes the wins believable.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: Vanity metrics
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
The most common way to talk about growth *badly* is the **vanity metric** — a number
that reliably goes up, feels like progress, and tells you nothing you can act on. Eric
Ries named the category in *The Lean Startup*, splitting metrics into *vanity* and
*actionable*, and the test he proposed is the one to keep: when this number moves, does
it tell me what to do differently? If not, it's vanity.

The usual suspects share a shape:

* **Cumulative totals.** Total registered users, signups ever, GMV to date. A running
  total can only go up — it literally cannot report a bad month — so it can't inform a
  single decision. a16z's [16 startup metrics](https://a16z.com/16-startup-metrics/) makes
  the point bluntly: show *active* users, not cumulative registered ones; the gap between
  the two is where the truth lives.
* **Raw reach.** Impressions, pageviews, follower counts. Big numbers that impress in a
  deck and disconnect entirely from value delivered — [Amplitude's definition](https://amplitude.com/blog/vanity-metrics)
  is that a vanity metric is one that can rise while the business goes nowhere.
* **Registered, not active.** "50,000 users" that's really 50,000 signups and 800
  weekly-actives. The number is true; the impression it creates is false.

Two tests catch all of them:

* **The "so what?" test.** After any number, ask: *what would I do differently if this were
  half, or double?* If there's no answer, it's context at best and theatre at worst. (This
  is the deletion test from the course's own writing voice, turned on your metrics.)
* **Ratio and cohort over cumulative count.** A vanity metric becomes actionable the
  moment you make it a *rate* (activation %, week-4 retention, paid-conversion %) or split
  it by *cohort* (how does the group that joined in May retain, vs April?). Rates can go
  *down*, which is exactly what makes them useful; cohorts show whether the product is
  getting better or you're just adding more people to a leaky bucket.

The trap is that vanity metrics aren't lies — "10,000 signups" is true. They're *true
numbers pointed away from the decision,* which is why they survive: defensible,
flattering, and everyone nods. The discipline is to lead with the ratio and keep the big
cumulative number where it belongs — as a trust signal for customers (§13.1), never as
evidence for the team. Chapter 4 built this honesty habit for reading your own dashboard;
this is the same habit applied to what you show other people.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: Growth theatre and over-claiming
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
Vanity metrics mislead by *choosing* the flattering number. Their close cousins mislead
by *overstating* what a real number proves. Two forms show up constantly.

**Growth theatre.** Activity performed because it *looks* like growth work — the referral
program launched for the announcement, the "growth hack" copied from a thread, the
dashboard built to be shown rather than read. It's the tactic collector from Chapter 0 in
a suit: constant motion, no stated leak, nothing measured against a control. The tell is
the same one — ask *which leak does this plug?* and there's no answer, only *it's what
growth teams do.*

**Over-claiming attribution.** The more corrosive form, because it wears the clothes of
rigour. "Our Product Hunt launch drove 10,000 signups." "The tweet that went viral got us
500 customers." These are **last-click hero stories** — they credit the final touch,
ignore everything that primed it, and almost always present a *correlational* number as if
it were *causal*. You already have the tool that exposes this (Chapter 5): what a channel
*reports* and what it actually *added* over doing nothing are different numbers, usually by
a lot — in the incrementality example from Chapter 5, reported ROAS of 2.31 against an
incremental 2.78. (Directional.) Sometimes reality is higher, often it's lower; it is
never automatically the reported figure.

Why under-claiming is the better long-run strategy:

* **Over-claiming has a compounding cost.** The first time a stakeholder catches a hero
  story that doesn't survive a holdout, *every* future number you present gets a mental
  discount. Credibility is the currency a report runs on, and it's priced by your least
  defensible claim, not your best one.
* **Attribution honesty is a differentiator.** "We think the launch helped, but we can't
  cleanly separate it from the press that week — here's what we *can* measure" sounds
  weaker and lands stronger. It signals you know the difference between correlation and
  cause, which is precisely what makes your causal claims — the holdout ones — believable.
* **Hold the hero stories to the holdout bar.** Not every number needs an experiment, but
  any number you're using to *justify spending more* does. If you're about to triple a
  budget on the strength of "it drove 10k signups," that is exactly the claim to run
  incrementality on first.

The through-line with vanity metrics: both are ways of talking about growth that feel good
in the room and cost you later. The antidote is the same one — say the smaller, defensible
number, and say plainly what you can't yet prove.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: The one report you share
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
Everything the course has built points at one artifact: a **single monthly one-pager** —
the board slide, the investor-update section, the thing you'd screenshot into the team
channel. If you can fill it honestly, the growth system is real. If you can't, it tells
you exactly which part isn't real yet.

It has four blocks and one sentence, in this order:

1. **North Star + trend.** The one metric that reflects value delivered (Chapter 2), shown
   as a *line over 3–6 months*, not a single number. The trend is the story; the point is
   trivia.
2. **Activation + retention.** The proof the bucket holds: activation rate for recent
   cohorts, and the retention curve or its key cohort number. This is the half of the page
   that says whether growth is real or churn-and-replace.
3. **Blended CAC + referral contribution.** The acquisition economics: what a customer
   costs blended across channels, and the share of new users arriving through the owned
   audience and referral loop (Chapter 8). Falling blended CAC as the owned audience grows
   is the flywheel showing up in the finances.
4. **One experiment learning.** Exactly one — stated as what you'll do differently, win or
   loss (§13.5). One real learning beats ten bullet points, because it proves the loop
   turned this month.

Plus the sentence most decks omit: **the one thing you're worried about.** For a board
especially, naming the risk you'd want help on builds more trust than a flawless slide —
it's the difference between reporting *to* your board and working *with* it.

Notice what's *not* on it: total registered users, emails sent, impressions, pageviews. A
one-pager that survives the "so what?" test on every line is the entire course expressed
as a single page someone else can read in two minutes — and it's the report the monthly
cadence in Chapter 9 exists to produce.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
Two things worth doing while the chapter is fresh:

1. **Fill the one-pager for real.** The template from §13.8 only earns its keep once it
   has your numbers in it — and the blanks you *can't* fill are the most valuable output,
   because they name next month's work precisely.
2. **A look at [your workbook](/workbook).** The board line, the two-number story, the two
   cadence skeletons, the holdout explanation, the honest attribution rewrite, and the
   one-pager draft now sit together — that collection *is* how you'll talk about growth,
   in one place, each entry linked back to the piece that produced it.

The chapter in one line: **decide the altitude before you pick the number, pair every
claim with its mechanism, and say the smaller, defensible thing.** Talking about growth
honestly isn't a soft skill bolted onto the real work — it's the last stage of the same
loop, and the one that decides whether anyone acts on what you found.

***

***

## Go deeper

* [a16z — 16 startup metrics](https://a16z.com/16-startup-metrics/) — the canonical list of what investors read, and the "know which number you mean" discipline behind the two-number story.
* [YC — Key startup metrics](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KR-key-startup-metrics) — what the outcome numbers on your one-pager eventually get measured against.
* [Brian Balfour — the weekly growth meeting](https://brianbalfour.com/essays/growth-meeting) — the fixed-agenda template the weekly-review skeleton compresses.
* [Reforge — operating cadence](https://www.reforge.com/blog/operating-cadence) — deciding what gets read daily, weekly, and monthly, and by whom.
* [Amplitude — vanity metrics](https://amplitude.com/blog/vanity-metrics) — the blunt definition: a number that rises while the business goes nowhere.
* [Eric Ries — The Lean Startup](http://theleanstartup.com/) — where the vanity-versus-actionable-metrics distinction comes from.
* [Eppo — marketing incrementality 101](https://www.geteppo.com/blog/marketing-incrementality-101) — the fuller version of "how do you know it worked," and why reported attribution overstates lift.
* [Lenny Rachitsky & Ronny Kohavi — the ultimate guide to A/B testing](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/the-ultimate-guide-to-ab-testing) — trust, invalid results, and why most experiments don't win.
* [First Round Review](https://review.firstround.com/) — an operator's library on board management and the metrics conversations founders most often get wrong.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: Chapter 13 — Talking about growth (and how not to)
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
> **In this chapter:** who cares about which number and at what altitude, why a single
> number never persuades and the two-number story that does, the two reporting cadences
> (weekly team review vs monthly board update), how to explain a holdout to a skeptic
> without a stats lecture, how to present a losing experiment as the learning you already
> paid for, how to spot the vanity metrics and growth-theatre that mislead, and how to
> assemble the one monthly report the whole course has been building toward. This is the
> human skill the loop depends on — the stage that decides whether anyone acts on what
> you found.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into nine short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read, each with a prompt or a checklist that turns the idea into something
in your workbook. Work through them in order, or jump to what you need:

1. [Who cares about what](./01-who-cares-about-what.mdx) — four audiences, four altitudes, and the one-report-for-all mistake.
2. [The two-number story](./02-the-two-number-story.mdx) — pair a leading metric with its lagging outcome; one number never persuades.
3. [Two cadences, two reports](./03-reporting-cadence.mdx) — the weekly team review vs the monthly board update, with a skeleton for each.
4. [How do you know it worked?](./04-explaining-incrementality.mdx) — explaining a holdout to a skeptic, without a stats lecture.
5. [Presenting a loss](./05-presenting-a-losing-experiment.mdx) — a losing experiment is a learning you already paid for.
6. [Vanity metrics](./06-vanity-metrics.mdx) — the numbers that impress and mislead, and the "so what?" test that catches them.
7. [Growth theatre and over-claiming](./07-growth-theatre-and-over-claiming.mdx) — performing growth, last-click hero stories, and the credibility cost.
8. [The one report you share](./08-the-one-report-you-share.mdx) — assemble the single monthly one-pager the whole course points at.
9. [Before you move on](./09-before-you-move-on.mdx) — recap, then a scenario quiz on the whole chapter.

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: What a growth capability is worth
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
This course taught you to run a loop: instrument a funnel, find where it leaks, ship a
journey, measure the lift honestly, then decide where the next hour goes. This chapter is
about what that's worth to your career — not as a salary figure this course won't invent,
but as leverage.

The useful frame is Cal Newport's **career capital**, from *So Good They Can't Ignore
You* ([calnewport.com](https://calnewport.com)): the rare and valuable skills you build
are the capital you trade for the things people actually want from work — autonomy,
interesting problems, a say in what you do next. His argument runs against the usual
advice: mastery tends to come *before* passion, not after it. You don't find a growth
role you love and then get good; you get good at running a loop, and the leverage that
buys is what makes the work feel like yours.

A growth capability is rare *because* it spans functions. Plenty of people can run one
channel well — write ads, send emails, tune a signup page. Far fewer can hold the whole
loop in their head: read the funnel, pick the leak with the most leverage, build the fix
across product and messaging, and prove it moved with a holdout instead of a vanity
chart. That span is exactly what makes it hard to hire for, and hard to fake.

It's also a genuine discipline, not a job title someone invented. Andrew Chen's 2012
essay "Growth Hacker is the new VP of Marketing"
([andrewchen.com](https://andrewchen.com)) named the shift: growth sits between product,
engineering, and marketing, and it's driven by data rather than taste. Product-led growth
widened the demand — a product-led company needs people who think in loops and can
instrument them, not just campaigns. Reforge ([reforge.com](https://www.reforge.com))
built a whole curriculum around treating growth as a repeatable system rather than a bag
of tricks, which is the same premise this course runs on.

That capability shows up under three main titles — growth PM, growth marketer, growth
engineer — three doors into the same loop, which the sixth piece decodes. What matters
here is the shape: a founder or growth leader values the person who can run the loop end
to end more than a specialist in any single stage of it, because that person replaces a
committee. The rest of this chapter turns your course work into the artifacts that prove
you're that person.

**What to take from it:**

* Verna's whole list is one argument: the growth "hacks" people chase — copy a competitor's
  onboarding, redesign the homepage, hire a head of growth to fix a slump — reliably fail,
  because growth *amplifies* a working loop rather than manufacturing one. The scarce skill
  is knowing that, which is loop-thinking, not tactic-collecting.
* Her highest-leverage move is building an *earned* channel you own — virality,
  word-of-mouth, user-generated content (Dropbox draws ~50% of acquisition from its sharing
  loop) — instead of renting attention from an algorithm. Seeing and building that loop is
  exactly the cross-functional capability this chapter calls career capital.
* Copying competitors, she says, is "the fastest way to mediocrity" — you can't lead by
  imitating. The operator who can read a funnel and design the fix, rather than reach for
  someone else's playbook, is the one who's hard to hire and hard to replace.

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: Your workbook is your portfolio
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
Most people applying for a growth role have a résumé that *asserts* things — "grew
activation," "owned lifecycle." You have something better: a record of the work itself.
The [workbook](/workbook) you filled in across this course is the raw material for a
portfolio, and a portfolio beats an assertion because it shows judgement, not just
outcomes.

Look at what's already in there. An activation sentence you defined and defended
(Chapter 2). A tracking plan — the events you chose to capture and why (Chapter 3). A
dashboard that reads the funnel (Chapter 4). Lifecycle journeys you actually shipped,
with the holdout results that say whether they moved anything (Chapters 5–6). A view on
where traffic should come from and how you'd measure it (Chapters 7–8). A 30/60/90 plan
(Chapter 10). That is the full loop, documented, with your reasoning attached — which is
exactly the thing an interviewer can't get from a bullet point.

The job now is compression. A portfolio nobody opens is worth nothing, so aim for a
single page (or one short doc, or a slide or two) that a hiring manager can read in two
minutes and a curious one can drill into. The shape that works: **the problem you saw in
the data → the fix you shipped → how you knew it worked (or didn't) → what you'd do
next.** That last clause matters more than it looks — showing what you learned from a
journey that *didn't* move the metric reads as more credible than an unbroken string of
wins, because real growth work is mostly negative results.

One honest caveat: your course workbook is built on your own product, or a practice one.
That's a strength — self-directed work on a real funnel is rarer than it sounds — but say
so plainly rather than dressing it up as something it isn't. Attribution humility (the
seventh piece) starts here, in how you frame your own evidence.

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: Project vs. loop
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
There's a line an experienced interviewer is listening for, usually without saying so out
loud: is this person telling me about a *project* they ran once, or a *loop* they run?
The difference decides a lot of growth offers, and it's worth understanding before you
tell your story.

**A project** has a start and an end. "We wanted more signups, so we ran a referral push,
and signups went up 20%." It's a fine thing to have done — but it's a one-off, and it
leaves the interviewer with questions the candidate didn't answer: up 20% versus what?
Would they have signed up anyway? What happened after? Did it repeat?

**A loop** is a system that keeps running. Same story, told as a loop: "New users weren't
reaching activation, so I instrumented the drop, shipped an onboarding journey, and held
out 10% to measure it. It lifted 30-day retention a few points against the holdout, so I
kept it and moved to the next-biggest leak — that measure-fix-measure cycle is how I
work." Notice what changed. There's a system, a control group, a decision about where to
go next, and a *cadence*. That's the growth team versus the team that did a growth
project once — the same distinction Chapter 9 draws.

Three things strong interviewers probe for, all of them ways of asking "loop or project?":

* **Systems thinking** — do you see the funnel as connected stages, or as isolated
  tactics? A loop-thinker fixes the leak with the most leverage; a project-thinker fixes
  whatever was asked for.
* **Measurement** — how did you *know* it worked? A number without a baseline is a story.
  A holdout, a control, a before/after with a comparison group is evidence.
* **Incrementality** — did the change cause the outcome, or ride along with it? This is
  the correlation-versus-causation discipline from Chapters 2 and 5, and it's the single
  most common place candidates over-claim (the seventh piece is about surviving that
  question).

You don't have to have run a loop for years to tell it as one. You ran one in this course:
measure → keep → grow, with holdouts and a plan. Frame it honestly at that scope and it
still reads as loop-thinking, which is the thing being tested.

A clean way to package one story is **STAR** — Situation, Task, Action, Result — a
long-standing behavioural-interview structure that keeps you concrete and stops you
drifting into generalities. Land the Result on a measured outcome and, if you can, the
decision it led to next. Draft yours here:

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: The 90-day plan as an artifact
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
A large share of growth and product interviews end on the same question, and it's often
the deciding one: *what would your first 90 days here look like?* Sometimes it's a
conversation; sometimes it's a written take-home or a presentation. Either way, you've
already built the artifact it's asking for — the 30/60/90 plan from Chapter 10 — and this
piece is about aiming that generic shape at a specific company.

Why interviewers lean on this question: a 90-day plan reveals in one page how a candidate
thinks. It shows whether you'll spend month one *looking* before acting, whether you
sequence retention before acquisition or reach straight for the tap, and whether you set
gates you can be held to. That's why the Chapter 10 plan works as an interview deliverable
almost unchanged — its whole spine is "learn the data first, find early wins in
activation and retention, only then scale acquisition," which is the same first-90-days
shape growth operators run when they land in a new role (Reforge's first-90-days guide
and First Round Review's [founder-led growth
playbook](https://review.firstround.com) both walk this arc).

What turns the generic plan into a *strong* answer is specificity to their business. Do
the homework the plan assumes:

* **Sign up as a real user.** Hit their onboarding, their paywall, their empty states —
  the way Chapter 10's first month is mostly looking. You'll walk in already knowing where
  their funnel probably leaks.
* **Name what you'd instrument first.** You can't promise results on data you haven't
  seen, so frame month one as measurement: "here's the funnel I'd want visible before I
  commit to projects." Restraint reads as maturity, not indecision.
* **Sequence honestly.** Put activation and retention work before paid scaling, and say
  why — the leaky-bucket argument is more persuasive than a promise to "drive growth."
* **Set gates, not just dates.** "By day 60 I'd want a holdout-measured lift on
  activation before we open the acquisition tap" tells them you measure before you spend.

The trap to avoid is the confident-stranger plan: bold targets and specific tactics for a
funnel you've never seen. It reads as someone who'll act before they look. A plan that
leads with what you'd *measure* — and admits what you can't know from the outside —
consistently beats one that promises a number on day one.

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: Interviewing for growth
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
Growth interviews vary by company, but the question types are stable enough to prepare
for. Knowing the shape lets you rehearse the substance instead of being surprised by the
format.

**What you'll be asked.** Four recurring types, each testing a different muscle:

* **Metrics fluency.** "What metrics would you watch for this product? Which is the North
  Star, and why?" They're checking whether you can pick a metric that reflects customer
  value and isn't a vanity number — the Chapter 2 material, live. Being able to say why
  revenue is a *lagging* score rather than the play separates fluency from jargon.
* **A past experiment.** "Walk me through a growth experiment you ran." This is the
  project-vs-loop test from the third piece — tell it as a loop, land the result on lift
  against a control.
* **A product teardown.** "Sign up for our product — where's the funnel leaking, and what
  would you fix first?" They want to see you *look* before you prescribe, and prioritise
  by leverage rather than by whatever's most visible.
* **A case study or take-home.** Bigger roles often include a written exercise or a
  presentation — frequently the 90-day plan from the fourth piece, or a "how would you
  grow X" prompt. Guides on preparing for PM and growth interviews (Lenny's Newsletter,
  [lennysnewsletter.com](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com)) consistently make the same
  point: judgement and structure beat a pile of tactics.

The throughline: in every one of these, **measurement judgement beats buzzwords**. The
candidate who says "I'd want a holdout before I claim that worked" outscores the one who
lists ten growth hacks, because the first sounds like someone who's been accountable for a
number and the second sounds like someone who's read about it.

**What you should ask them.** The questions you ask are themselves a signal — they reveal
whether you think like an operator. Strong ones probe the conditions you'd need to
actually do the job: *Is the product instrumented, and will I have access to the data? Who
owns the growth loop today? What's been tried, and how did you know whether it worked? Is
growth in the same backlog as product, or a separate function?* Their answers also tell
*you* whether the role is real (the sixth piece is the field guide to reading those
signals).

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: Reading a growth job description
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
"Growth" on a job posting covers at least three different jobs, and telling them apart
before you apply saves you from interviewing for a role you didn't want. The same title
can mean a data-driven loop-owner at one company and a cold-email operator at another, so
read the responsibilities, not the header.

**The three shapes of a growth role.** They're three doors into the same loop, weighted
differently:

* **Growth marketer** — leans on the acquisition and top-of-funnel stages: channels,
  campaigns, messaging, landing pages. The best ones still measure and run experiments;
  the weakest postings mean "do marketing tasks" with *growth* bolted on for appeal.
* **Growth PM** — owns a slice of the funnel as a product surface: activation,
  onboarding, retention, monetisation. Runs the experiment backlog, works across
  engineering and design, lives in the metrics. Closest to the whole-loop role.
* **Growth engineer** — builds the machinery: instrumentation, experimentation tooling,
  the pipelines and internal surfaces that let the loop run fast. If you came to this
  course as an engineer, this is often the most natural door.

**Green flags** — signs the loop is real:

* A described loop, not a list of channels — the posting talks about measuring, iterating,
  and retention, not just "drive signups."
* Explicit **data access** and instrumentation. If the role owns a metric, you need to be
  able to see it; a posting that mentions the analytics stack is telling you the loop
  exists.
* Experimentation as a norm — A/B tests, holdouts, a backlog. It signals a team that
  knows the difference between activity and lift.
* Growth sits with product and engineering, not stranded in a marketing silo.

**Red flags** — signs it's a channel job wearing a growth costume:

* *Growth = cold outreach only.* If the whole role is outbound volume with no analytics,
  it's a sales-development job relabelled.
* No mention of data, metrics, or measurement anywhere in the responsibilities.
* "Growth hacking" as vibe — a wish-list of tactics with no system, no ownership of a
  number, no instrumentation.
* Everything is acquisition; nothing about activation or retention. That's the leaky-bucket
  team from Chapter 2, hiring someone to pour faster.

Reforge's writing on growth roles and org structure
([reforge.com](https://www.reforge.com)) is the fullest map of how these functions differ
in practice, if you want to go deeper on which door fits you. The short version: match the
posting's *verbs* to the stage of the loop you most want to own, and treat data access as
non-negotiable — a growth role without it is a growth role in name only.

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: Attribution humility
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
There's a temptation, when you're selling yourself, to round your impact up. "I grew
activation 3x." "My campaign drove the quarter." Resist it — because the person across the
table has almost certainly run these numbers themselves, and they know how hard
attribution really is. Over-claiming doesn't read as confidence; it reads as someone
who's never been accountable for a number they had to defend.

This is the same honesty the course has argued for throughout, now pointed at your own
story. Chapter 13's whole subject is that attribution is hard: multiple things move at
once, seasonality and the base rate move on their own, and a change that *correlates* with
a metric didn't necessarily *cause* it — the correlation-versus-causation discipline from
Chapters 2 and 5. If you internalised that for your product, apply it to your résumé too.

The credible move is to pair any claim with **how you'd know**. Compare:

* *Over-claim:* "I increased retention 40%."
* *Honest, and stronger:* "We shipped an onboarding journey and held out 10% of new users.
  30-day retention ran a few points higher in the treated group over two months. I'd want
  a longer window and a repeat before I called it durable, but the holdout gives me
  confidence the journey caused most of it."

The second version is longer and it's *more* persuasive, because everything in it is
checkable. It shows you know what a holdout is for, that you distinguish lift from
activity, and that you know the limits of your own evidence. To a growth leader, that's
the whole job — you're going to be trusted to spend money and report on it, and they're
hiring the person who won't fool them or themselves.

Saying "I don't know for certain, but here's how I'd find out" is not a weakness in a
growth interview. It's the single clearest signal that you've actually done the work,
because only people who've measured things honestly know how much they don't know. The
candidate claiming a clean 10x with no control group is telling you they haven't.

Take one line from your own story — the one you were tempted to round up — and rewrite it
as the honest version:

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: Build your own audience
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
Chapter 8 made the case that a business should build an audience it *owns* — an email
list, a community, a direct line to its users — rather than renting attention from a
platform that can change the rules. The same logic applies to your career, and it's a
fitting note to close the course on.

An owned audience for *you* is the people who know what you can do because you've shown
them — through writing, building in public, a newsletter, talks, or just a habit of
posting what you're learning. It's a career flywheel: the work you do generates things
worth sharing, sharing them reaches people, and some of those people bring opportunities —
which generate more work worth sharing. Andrew Chen's own trajectory
([andrewchen.com](https://andrewchen.com)) is a clean example of the pattern — years of
writing about growth became the platform for everything that followed. This isn't a claim
that everyone must become a creator; it's that a small, genuine audience compounds
quietly in the background of a career, and it costs little to start.

Why it works is the same reason it worked for your product. You **own the relationship** —
it doesn't depend on a recruiter's inbox or a job board's algorithm. It's **compounding** —
a body of writing keeps working while you sleep, the way a retained user keeps paying
back their acquisition cost. And it's **differentiating** — most candidates are a résumé;
someone who's visibly done and explained the work stands out before the interview starts.

You don't need a large following for this to pay off. A handful of the right people —
founders, growth leaders, peers who'd vouch for you — is worth more than a big anonymous
number, which is exactly the owned-audience-over-vanity-metric lesson from earlier in the
course, applied to you. And you already have the raw material: the portfolio from the
second piece, the honest write-ups of what worked and what didn't from the seventh. The
first move is small — publish one thing.

**What to take from it:**

* Robinson's core move is being useful in public: show what's possible, then teach people
  how to build it themselves. Trust compounds over many touches — his skeptical audience,
  developers, tends to adopt around the *tenth* time they hear about something, not the
  first — which is why a small owned audience pays back slowly, then all at once.
* Writing is the compounding asset, and his process makes it repeatable: ideas sit as
  bullets in a notes app until "this needs to be in the world," a rough first draft,
  feedback from people you trust, then a rewrite that moves the conclusion to the top. A
  tweet is the small version that later expands into the post — start smaller than a post.
* Write like you talk: cut jargon and hedging, lead with the bottom line, keep it
  skimmable. The point isn't polish — it's being genuinely useful, which is what earns the
  handful of right people (this atom's owned audience) over a vanity follower count.

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
The through-line of this chapter: the loop you ran in this course is career capital, and
the job of this last chapter was to repackage it into evidence someone hires on. You have
a portfolio (your workbook), a story told as a loop rather than a project (STAR), a
first-90-days plan aimed at a real company, an honest way to talk about impact, and a plan
to start building an audience of your own. Everything you wrote is in
[your workbook](/workbook), each entry linked back to the piece that produced it — that
page is your growth portfolio now.

Check yourself on the whole chapter:

***

## Go deeper

* [Cal Newport — So Good They Can't Ignore You](https://calnewport.com) — the "career capital" argument this chapter's title borrows: build rare skills first, trade them for control over your work.
* [Reforge](https://www.reforge.com) — the fullest map of growth as a repeatable discipline, and of how growth-marketing, growth-PM, and growth-engineering roles differ in practice.
* [Andrew Chen](https://andrewchen.com) — growth as a cross-functional discipline ("Growth Hacker is the new VP of Marketing"), and a living example of an owned audience compounding into a career.
* [Lenny's Newsletter](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com) — practical guides on growth and product careers, interviewing, and case studies; the reference most operators point new candidates to.
* [First Round Review](https://review.firstround.com) — operator-written pieces on founder-led growth and early growth hiring, useful for calibrating what a first-90-days plan should show.

***

This is the last chapter of the course. You started by learning to measure a funnel; you
finish able to run the whole loop — measure, keep, grow — and to trade that capability for
the kind of work you want. The machine you built runs on your product now, and the
evidence that you can build it is in [your workbook](/workbook). Go publish one thing.

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: Chapter 14 — Growth as career capital
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
> **In this chapter:** what a growth capability is worth now and why full-loop operators
> are scarce, how the workbook you filled in across this course is already a portfolio,
> the difference between "I ran a growth project" and "I run a growth loop" and how to
> tell your story so it reads as the latter, turning your 30/60/90 plan into an interview
> deliverable, the questions you'll be asked (and should ask), decoding a growth job
> description, talking about your impact without over-claiming, and applying the course's
> own owned-audience lesson to your own career. By the end you'll have the artifacts and
> the language to trade what you learned here for a role.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into nine short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. This is the last chapter of the course, so it's less about new
technique and more about repackaging what you already did into evidence someone will
hire on. Several pieces end with something to write; those save to your workbook next to
the rest of your work. Go in order, or jump to what you need:

1. [What a growth capability is worth](./01-what-its-worth.mdx) — rare skills you can trade for good work.
2. [Your workbook is your portfolio](./02-your-workbook-is-your-portfolio.mdx) — the loop you built is already the evidence.
3. [Project vs. loop](./03-project-vs-loop.mdx) — the one distinction strong interviewers probe for.
4. [The 90-day plan as an artifact](./04-the-90-day-plan-as-artifact.mdx) — turn Chapter 10 into an interview deliverable.
5. [Interviewing for growth](./05-interviewing-for-growth.mdx) — the questions you'll be asked, and the ones to ask.
6. [Reading a growth job description](./06-reading-a-growth-jd.mdx) — green flags, red flags, and what titles really mean.
7. [Attribution humility](./07-attribution-humility.mdx) — why the honest candidate beats the one claiming 10x.
8. [Build your own audience](./08-build-your-own-audience.mdx) — the owned-audience lesson, applied to you.
9. [Before you move on](./09-before-you-move-on.mdx) — a recap, a quiz on the whole chapter, and where to read more.
